# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Tyre Design Based on End-Use Requirement

### Formal Academic Definition
**Tyre Design** is a systematic engineering process involving the selection of tyre architecture (size, ply rating, aspect ratio, tread pattern, etc.) and the formulation of rubber compounds for each functional component, such that the finished product meets a pre-defined set of performance criteria (load, speed, terrain, durability, rolling resistance, wet grip, etc.) as dictated by its intended application.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive:** Students are expected to *design a tyre for a specific end-use* and prepare a *compounding plan* listing ingredients and formulations for the various tyre components. This is an **application-based / design-oriented** learning outcome (mapped to **CO1 / CO2** of the syllabus).

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine you are a **chef preparing a multi-course meal** for different guests:
- A **bodybuilder** needs a high-protein, low-carb meal → analogously, a **truck tyre** needs high load-bearing, low heat-build-up compounds.
- A **sprinter** needs fast-energy carbohydrates → analogously, a **racing tyre** needs high-grip, soft tread compounds.
- A **mountain hiker** needs sturdy, weatherproof gear → analogously, an **off-road tyre** needs cut-resistant, high-tear-strength sidewalls.

In each case, you don't use the *same recipe* — you tailor the **ingredients (polymers, fillers, plasticizers, curatives)** and the **cooking process (mixing, curing time, temperature, pressure)** to the *intended end-use*.

### Key Design Parameters (End-Use Driven)
| End-Use Application | Load Index (Typical) | Speed Symbol | Primary Design Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|
| Passenger Car (PCR) | 91–105 | T–H | Comfort, low rolling resistance, wet grip |
| Light Commercial (LT) | 108–121 | R–T | Load + durability balance |
| Truck & Bus (TBR) | 140–160 | J–L | High load, low heat build-up, retreadability |
| Agricultural | 110–140 | A6–D | Traction, cut/chip resistance, flex fatigue |
| Earthmover (OTR) | 150+ | F–G | Cut resistance, ozone, heat dissipation |
| Racing / High-Performance | 85–100 | W–Y | Maximum grip, steering response |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metric:** Tyre design is governed by standards such as **ETRTO (European Tyre and Rim Technical Organisation)**, **TRA (Tyre & Rim Association, USA)**, and **JATMA (Japan Automobile Tyre Manufacturers Association)**.

---

## 1.2 Anatomy of a Pneumatic Tyre — The 7 Functional Components

A modern radial tyre is a **composite laminate** made of **rubber compounds**, **textile cords**, and **steel belts**, each engineered for a specific function. Every component uses a *different rubber formulation*.

```
            ┌─────────────────────────────┐
            │        TREAD CAP            │  ← Road contact, grip, wear
            │   (Cap + Base compound)     │
            ├─────────────────────────────┤
            │       BELT PACK (Steel)     │  ← Stability, puncture resistance
            ├─────────────────────────────┤
            │    INNER LINER (Halobutyl)  │  ← Air retention
            ├─────────────────────────────┤
            │       CARCASS PLIES         │  ← Load-bearing, flex
            │   (Polyester / Rayon)       │
            ├─────────────────────────────┤
            │       BEAD APEX / FILLER    │  ← Bead stiffening
            ├═════════════════════════════┤
            │   BEAD CORE (Steel Wire)    │  ← Rim seating
            └─────────────────────────────┘
              ↑                         ↑
          SIDEWALL (Left)        SIDEWALL (Right)
          Ozone/weathering        Ozone/weathering
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A *single tyre contains 8 to 12 distinct rubber compounds*, each with its own formulation. This is the heart of the design exercise.

# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULATION SHEET

## 2.1 The Compounding Design Process — Step-by-Step Logic

Designing a rubber compound for a tyre component is a **constrained multi-objective optimization** problem. The formulator balances 6 to 10 performance properties simultaneously.

### Step 1: Define the Performance Window
Ask the key questions:
1. **What functional job does this component do?** (grip / seal air / carry load / flex)
2. **What is the operating temperature range?**
3. **What is the strain / deformation cycle?**
4. **What is the expected service life?**
5. **What is the cost ceiling (in ₹/kg)?**

### Step 2: Select the Base Elastomer(s)
The **base elastomer** is the single most important decision — it dictates 60–70% of the compound's properties.

### Step 3: Select the Reinforcement System
Carbon black, silica, or hybrid — chosen for tensile strength, abrasion resistance, hysteresis.

### Step 4: Select the Plasticizer / Processing Aid
Aromatic oils, paraffinic oils, ester plasticizers — chosen for processability and low-Tg flexibility.

### Step 5: Select the Cure System
Sulphur + accelerators (conventional) or peroxide / resin cure (specialty).

### Step 6: Select the Antidegradant Package
Antioxidants, antiozonants, waxes — chosen for service environment.

### Step 7: Optimize the Ratio and Validate
Use **phr (parts per hundred rubber)** as the formulation language. Run lab tests (Mooney, MDR, tensile, hardness) and iterate.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formulation Sheet — Typical Compound Recipes (in **phr**)

> [!NOTE]
> **phr = parts per hundred rubber.** All ingredient quantities are normalized to 100 parts of base elastomer. This is the *universal language* of rubber compounding.

### Table 2A: Master Formulation Table for Major Tyre Components

| Ingredient (Function) | Tread (PCR) | Sidewall | Inner Liner | Carcass (Ply) | Bead Apex | Belt Skim | Chafer |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Natural Rubber (NR)** | 30 | 50 | — | 100 | 80 | 70 | 60 |
| **S-SBR (Solution SBR)** | 70 | — | — | — | — | — | — |
| **E-SBR** | — | 30 | — | — | — | — | — |
| **Polybutadiene (BR)** | — | 20 | — | — | 20 | 30 | 20 |
| **Bromobutyl (BIIR)** | — | — | 100 | — | — | — | — |
| **Carbon Black N330** | 35 | — | — | — | — | — | — |
| **Carbon Black N550** | — | 40 | — | 35 | 50 | 50 | 45 |
| **Carbon Black N660** | — | — | 60 | 25 | — | — | — |
| **Silica (precipitated)** | 30 | — | — | — | — | — | — |
| **Silane Coupling Agent (TESPT)** | 6 | — | — | — | — | — | — |
| **Aromatic Oil (TDAE)** | 25 | 12 | 8 | 6 | 6 | 8 | 10 |
| **Zinc Oxide** | 3 | 4 | 3 | 5 | 5 | 4 | 4 |

| **CBS (accelerator)** | 1.5 | 1.0 | — | 1.0 | 0.8 | 1.0 | 1.0 |
| **TBBS** | 0.5 | 0.5 | — | — | — | — | — |
| **DPG** | 1.5 | — | — | — | — | — | — |

| **Microcrystalline Wax** | 1.0 | 2.5 | — | 1.0 | 0.5 | 1.0 | 1.0 |
| **Cobalt Naphthenate** | — | — | — | 1.0 | — | 1.0 | 0.5 |
| **Resorcinol-Hexamine (R/H)** | — | — | — | 3.5 | — | 4.0 | 3.0 |
| **Total phr (approx.)** | **210** | **168** | **173** | **180** | **170** | **175** | **158** |

---

### Table 2B: Why Each Component Needs a Different Formulation — The Logic

| Component | Functional Demand | Dominant Property Required | Polymer Choice Logic | Filler Choice Logic |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Tread (Cap)** | Road contact, grip, wear | High wet grip + low rolling resistance | S-SBR (silica compatible) + NR | Silica + silane for low hysteresis |
| **Tread (Base)** | Cool running, adhesion to belt | Low heat build-up, good adhesion | NR + BR blend | High-structure CB N330 |
| **Sidewall** | Flex + weather resistance | Ozone + flex fatigue resistance | NR + EPDM blend or NR/BR | N550 (semi-reinforcing) |
| **Inner Liner** | Air retention | Air impermeability | Halogenated butyl (BIIR/CIIR) | N660 + platy fillers |
| **Carcass (Ply skim)** | Load, flex, cord adhesion | Green strength + adhesion | 100% NR (high green strength) | N550 + N660, cobalt + R/H system |
| **Bead Apex** | Bead stiffening + ride | High stiffness, low heat | NR + BR | N550 (high loading) |
| **Belt Skim** | Steel cord adhesion, stiffness | Adhesion to brass-coated steel | NR rich | N550, cobalt, R/H |
| **Chafer** | Rim abrasion, bead protection | Abrasion resistance | NR + SBR | N550/N660 high loading |

---

### Table 2C: Key Material Selection Rules (High-Yield for KTU)

| Rule No. | Selection Principle | Engineering Reason |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Higher Tg polymer → better wet grip, worse fuel economy** | Hysteresis is the cost of grip |
| 2 | **Silica + silane → lower rolling resistance than carbon black** | Silica–rubber coupling is reversible, absorbs energy selectively |
| 3 | **NR is irreplaceable in carcass & bead** | High green strength (uncured) for ply stitching |
| 4 | **BIIR is irreplaceable in inner liner** | Air permeability of BIIR ≈ 1/10 of NR |
| 5 | **Antiozonant (6PPD) + wax → outdoor weather protection** | Wax blooms to surface, 6PPD scavenges ozone |
| 6 | **Higher sulphur → more polysulphidic crosslinks → better fatigue** | Polysulphidic bonds break and re-form reversibly |
| 7 | **Cobalt + R/H → steel cord adhesion** | Cobalt forms CoS, R/H forms resorcinol-formaldehyde resin |
| 8 | **Plasticizer (oil) controls hardness and processability** | More oil → lower Mooney, lower hardness |

---

### Table 2D: Key Process Parameters That Accompany the Formulation

| Parameter | Tread | Sidewall | Inner Liner | Carcass |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mixer Type** | Intermeshing (tangential) | Tangential | Tangential | Intermeshing |
| **Mix Temp (Dump °C)** | 150–160 | 140–150 | 130–140 | 145–155 |
| **Cure Temp (°C)** | 160 | 165 | 170 | 160 |
| **Cure Time (min)** | 12–18 | 10–15 | 8–12 | 12–16 |
| **Mooney Viscosity ML(1+4)@100°C** | 55–70 | 50–60 | 45–55 | 60–75 |
| **Shore A Hardness** | 60–70 | 50–60 | 45–55 | 55–65 |

---

## 2.3 Engineering Trade-Off Visualization

The **Magic Triangle** of tyre performance — the formulator's nightmare:

```
                 WET GRIP
                    ▲
                   /│\
                  / │ \
                 /  │  \
                /   │   \
               /    │    \
              /     │     \
             /      │      \
            /       │       \
           ◀────────┼────────▶
   ROLLING                  WEAR /
 RESISTANCE                 TREAD
                            LIFE
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> You can optimize **any two**, but the **third suffers**. This is why different tyre SKUs (summer / winter / EV / performance) exist — they bias the triangle differently.

# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: The Compound Cost Calculation

One of the most common KTU numerical questions is **cost calculation of a rubber compound** from a given formulation. Let us derive the procedure step by step.

### Given:
A tread compound formulation with the following data:

| Ingredient | phr | Price (₹/kg) |
|---|---|---|
| S-SBR | 70 | 180 |
| NR (RSS-4) | 30 | 220 |
| Silica | 30 | 90 |
| Silane (TESPT) | 6 | 600 |
| TDAE Oil | 25 | 110 |
| 6PPD | 2.0 | 450 |
| ZnO | 3.0 | 250 |
| Stearic Acid | 2.0 | 150 |
| Sulphur | 1.8 | 50 |
| CBS | 1.5 | 600 |
| DPG | 1.5 | 350 |
| TMQ | 1.0 | 400 |
| Wax | 1.0 | 200 |

### To Find:
Compound cost in **₹/kg** of finished compound.

---

### Step 1: Compute Total phr

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total phr} &= 70 + 30 + 30 + 6 + 25 + 2.0 + 3.0 + 2.0 + 1.8 + 1.5 + 1.5 + 1.0 + 1.0 \\
&= 174.8 \text{ phr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic:** Every ingredient in the recipe is normalized to "parts per 100 parts of base rubber." The total phr includes both the rubber (100 phr) and the additives. This total phr represents the weight in grams of compound produced from 100 g of base rubber.

---

### Step 2: Compute Cost Contribution of Each Ingredient

The cost contribution of an ingredient is:

$$
C_i = \frac{\text{phr}_i \times \text{Price}_i}{1000} \quad \text{(₹ per 100 g of base rubber)}
$$

For example, for S-SBR:

$$
C_{\text{S-SBR}} = \frac{70 \times 180}{1000} = 12.60 \text{ ₹}
$$

---

### Step 3: Tabulate All Cost Contributions

| Ingredient | phr | Price (₹/kg) | Cost Contribution (₹) |
|---|---|---|---|
| S-SBR | 70 | 180 | 12.600 |
| NR | 30 | 220 | 6.600 |
| Silica | 30 | 90 | 2.700 |
| Silane | 6 | 600 | 3.600 |
| TDAE Oil | 25 | 110 | 2.750 |
| 6PPD | 2.0 | 450 | 0.900 |
| ZnO | 3.0 | 250 | 0.750 |
| Stearic Acid | 2.0 | 150 | 0.300 |
| Sulphur | 1.8 | 50 | 0.090 |
| CBS | 1.5 | 600 | 0.900 |
| DPG | 1.5 | 350 | 0.525 |
| TMQ | 1.0 | 400 | 0.400 |
| Wax | 1.0 | 200 | 0.200 |
| **Total** | **174.8** | — | **32.315** |

---

### Step 4: Compute Compound Cost per kg

The total cost of ₹32.315 corresponds to **174.8 g** of finished compound. Therefore, cost per gram is:

$$
\text{Cost per g} = \frac{32.315}{174.8} = 0.1849 \text{ ₹/g}
$$

Cost per kg of finished compound:

$$
\boxed{\text{Compound Cost} = 0.1849 \times 1000 = 184.90 \text{ ₹/kg}}
$$

---

### Step 5: Sanity Check (Rule of Thumb)
A typical tread compound costs **₹170–₹220 per kg** in India (2024 prices). Our answer of **₹184.90/kg** lies in the expected range. ✔

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Hardness Estimation from Oil and Filler Loading

A useful rule of thumb used by compounders:

$$
\text{Shore A} \approx 40 + (0.85 \times \text{CB phr}) - (0.45 \times \text{Oil phr}) + (\text{Base polymer hardness term})
$$

For the tread above (assume base polymer term = 30):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Shore A} &\approx 40 + (0.85 \times 35) - (0.45 \times 25) + 30 \\
&= 40 + 29.75 - 11.25 + 30 \\
&\approx 88.5
\end{aligned}
$$

This overestimates because it ignores the silica contribution. In practice, **silica at 30 phr gives lower hardness than 30 phr CB** because of lower structure and density. The formulator would adjust by **+2 to +4 points correction**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU exam tip: If asked "estimate hardness", show the **dominant terms** (filler loading and oil) — full accuracy is not required, but the *direction* of effect must be correct.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical: Loading Selection for a TBR Carcass Compound

**Problem:** For a TBR (Truck-Bus Radial) carcass skim, a compounder must achieve:
- Hardness: 60 ± 3 Shore A
- Tensile: ≥ 22 MPa
- Elongation at break: ≥ 500%
- Good green tack for ply building
- Cobalt-activated steel/rubber adhesion (if steel carcass)

**Compounding choice:**

| Slot | Ingredient | phr | Justification |
|---|---|---|---|
| Base | NR (CV-grade) | 100 | High green strength + tack |
| Filler | N550 | 45 | Semi-reinforcing, low heat |
| Filler | N660 | 15 | Processability, extrusion smoothness |
| Plasticizer | TDAE Oil | 6 | Low loading — preserve tack |
| Adhesion | Cobalt Naphthenate | 1.0 | Steel-cord adhesion promoter |
| Adhesion | Resorcinol | 2.5 | R/H resin precursor |
| Adhesion | Hexamine (HMT) | 1.5 | Resin crosslinker |
| Cure | ZnO | 5 | Cure activator |
| Cure | Stearic Acid | 2 | ZnO activator + dispersant |
| Cure | Sulphur | 2.5 | High sulphur for fatigue life |
| Cure | CBS | 0.9 | Delayed-action accelerator |
| Cure | TBBS | 0.3 | Scorch safety |
| AO | 6PPD | 1.5 | Antiozonant |
| AO | TMQ | 1.0 | Heat-ageing antioxidant |
| Process | Wax | 1.0 | Blooming agent for ozone |

**Total phr = 184.7**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why no SBR or BR in carcass?** Because S-SBR/BR compounds have **poor green strength** — the uncured ply would distort during tyre building. NR's strain-induced crystallization gives the *self-reinforcing* green strength.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** 
**List the major components of a pneumatic radial tyre and state the dominant functional property required of the rubber compound in each component.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The major components and their dominant property requirements are:

1. **Tread (Cap + Base):** Wear resistance, wet grip, low rolling resistance, low heat build-up.
2. **Sidewall:** Ozone resistance, flex fatigue resistance, weather resistance.
3. **Carcass (ply skim):** High green strength, ply-to-ply adhesion, flex endurance.
4. **Inner liner:** Air impermeability (BIIR dominates).
5. **Belt skim:** Steel-cord adhesion, high stiffness, low heat.
6. **Bead apex:** High stiffness for bead wedge action, low hysteresis.
7. **Chafer / Toe strip:** Rim chafe resistance, abrasion resistance.

> [!TIP]
> **Marking scheme:** ½ mark per component (max 6 components) + ½ mark for the dominant property. Aim for crisp, one-line answers per component.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**Why is Bromobutyl Rubber (BIIR) chosen as the base elastomer for the inner liner of a tubeless tyre? Justify with at least two technical reasons.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

BIIR is selected because:

1. **Air impermeability:** The air permeability of BIIR is **~5 × 10⁻⁸ cm³·cm/cm²·s·cmHg** — about **8 to 10 times lower** than natural rubber. For a tubeless tyre operating at 30–35 psi over 5+ years, only halobutyl keeps pressure loss within **<2 psi/month**.
2. **Cure compatibility:** BIIR can be co-cured with the carcass compound (NR-rich) at 160°C because of its reactive bromine site, allowing monolithic tyre cure.
3. **Flexibility at low temperature:** Maintains flexibility at –30 °C, essential for winter markets.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks):**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Design a rubber compound formulation (in **phr**) for the **tread compound of a passenger car radial (PCR) tyre** intended for the Indian market, with emphasis on **fuel economy and wet grip**. Justify the choice of each major ingredient (elastomer, filler, plasticizer, cure system, antidegradant). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A compounder wants to replace 20 phr of carbon black N330 with silica + silane in a tread compound to reduce rolling resistance. Compute:
- (i) the new total phr,
- (ii) the percentage change in compound cost, and
- (iii) the expected change in wet-grip index (use the rule: every 1 phr of silica replacing CB → +1.5% wet grip; every 1 phr of silane → –0.5% in compound cost per phr-equivalent handling). Given: N330 = ₹95/kg, Silica = ₹90/kg, Silane TESPT = ₹600/kg. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q-A(a):**

| Ingredient | phr | Justification (Mapped to Marks) |
|---|---|---|
| S-SBR (solution SBR) | 70 | High Tg (–30 °C), best wet grip; polymerized with functional groups for silica coupling |
| NR (RSS-4) | 30 | Improves tear strength, chip resistance, green tack for tread extrusion |
| Silica (precipitated) | 50 | Reduces rolling resistance hysteresis by ~20% vs CB-only systems; improves wet grip |
| Silane TESPT | 5 | Coupling agent — bonds silica to rubber, prevents filler–filler agglomeration |
| TDAE Oil | 25 | Low-PAH, REACH-compliant plasticizer; controls Mooney viscosity, lowers Tg flex |
| 6PPD | 2.0 | Antiozonant for sidewall crack resistance (also helps tread base) |
| TMQ | 1.0 | Heat-ageing antioxidant — protects tread under high-speed running |
| ZnO | 3.0 | Cure activator (forms ZnS during vulcanization) |
| Stearic Acid | 2.0 | Solubilizes ZnO; processing aid |
| Sulphur | 1.8 | Crosslinker — polysulphidic bonds for fatigue life |
| CBS | 1.5 | Primary accelerator — delayed action, good scorch safety |
| DPG | 1.5 | Secondary accelerator — activates silica/silane system |
| Wax | 1.0 | Bloom agent for ozone protection |
| **Total** | **~193.8** | |

**Justification Highlights (3 most important):**
1. S-SBR/BR/NR blend gives the **magic triangle balance**.
2. Silica + silane system is the **gold standard for low RR + wet grip**.
3. CBS + DPG + low sulphur → **efficient cure network** with delayed onset.

> [!NOTE]
> **Validation:** Target hardness 65 ± 3 Shore A, Tensile ≥ 18 MPa, Elongation ≥ 450%, DIN abrasion ≤ 120 mm³.

---

#### **Solution to Q-A(b):**

**Given:**
- Original: N330 at 35 phr, ₹95/kg
- Replacement: 20 phr silica (₹90/kg) + 2 phr silane (₹600/kg)
- (Replacing only 20 of the 35 phr — leaving 15 phr CB for tear strength)

**(i) New total phr calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Removed:} &\quad 20 \text{ phr CB} \\
\text{Added:} &\quad 20 \text{ phr Silica} + 2 \text{ phr Silane} = 22 \text{ phr} \\
\Delta\text{phr} &= +2 \text{ phr} \\
\text{New Total} &\approx 193.8 + 2 = 195.8 \text{ phr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Cost change:**

Cost removed per 100 g rubber:

$$
\frac{20 \times 95}{1000} = 1.90 \text{ ₹}
$$

Cost added per 100 g rubber:

$$
\frac{20 \times 90 + 2 \times 600}{1000} = \frac{1800 + 1200}{1000} = 3.00 \text{ ₹}
$$

Net cost change:

$$
\Delta C = +3.00 - 1.90 = +1.10 \text{ ₹ per 100 g rubber}
$$

Original total compound cost per 100 g rubber ≈ ₹32.315 (from Section 3.1).

Percentage change:

$$
\% \Delta C = \frac{+1.10}{32.315} \times 100 \approx +3.40\%
$$

**(iii) Wet-grip index change:**

$$
\Delta \text{Grip} = 20 \times (+1.5\%) = +30\% \text{ (relative improvement)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistake:** Confusing **per phr replacement** with **per phr addition**. The replacement of 20 phr CB *adds 20 phr silica* — both are added to the compound. The 15 phr CB is *retained*, not removed.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice:**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** For a **Truck-Bus Radial (TBR) tyre carcass skim compound**, propose a complete formulation (in phr) and explain why **100% Natural Rubber is irreplaceable** in this application. Discuss the role of cobalt naphthenate and the resorcinol-hexamine (R/H) system. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the **inner liner compound of a tubeless radial tyre** with the **sidewall compound** in terms of (i) base elastomer choice, (ii) filler system, (iii) antidegradant package, and (iv) cure system. Tabulate the comparison and justify each choice with a one-line reason. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Q-B(a):**

**Proposed TBR Carcass Skim Formulation:**

| Ingredient | phr | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| NR (CV-60 grade) | 100 | High green strength, strain-induced crystallization |
| Carbon Black N550 | 45 | Semi-reinforcing, low heat, good adhesion |
| Carbon Black N660 | 15 | Processability, smooth extrusion |
| TDAE Oil | 6 | Low plasticizer to preserve tack |
| Cobalt Naphthenate | 1.0 | Promotes CuS formation at brass–rubber interface |
| Resorcinol | 2.5 | Forms RF resin with hexamine |
| Hexamine (HMT) | 1.5 | Crosslinks RF resin in situ |
| ZnO | 5 | Cure activator |
| Stearic Acid | 2 | Dispersant + activator |
| Sulphur | 2.5 | High loading for fatigue life |
| CBS | 0.9 | Delayed-action accelerator |
| TBBS | 0.3 | Scorch safety |
| 6PPD | 1.5 | Antiozonant |
| TMQ | 1.0 | Antioxidant |
| Wax | 1.0 | Ozone barrier |
| **Total** | **184.7** | |

**Why 100% NR is irreplaceable (4 key reasons — 4 marks):**
1. **Green strength** — strain-induced crystallization gives NR the ability to hold its shape during the *uncured* tyre-building stage; SBR/BR would slump and distort.
2. **Tack** — NR has the highest auto-adhesion (tack) of all elastomers; critical for ply-to-ply bonding during building.
3. **Fatigue life** — NR exhibits the highest cut-growth resistance under cyclic flexing.
4. **Adhesion to textile and steel** — NR is the universal adhesion-friendly matrix; cobalt + R/H systems work only in NR-rich matrices.

**Role of Cobalt Naphthenate (1.5 marks):**
- Cobalt migrates to the brass–rubber interface during cure.
- Promotes the formation of a thin, tenacious **CuS (copper sulphide) layer** — the actual chemical bond.
- Without cobalt, the CuS layer is too thick and brittle → poor adhesion.

**Role of Resorcinol–Hexamine (R/H) system (1.5 marks):**
- Resorcinol + hexamine react *in situ* to form a **resorcinol–formaldehyde (RF) resin**.
- The RF resin **crosslinks the rubber near the cord**, increasing the modulus of the rubber *close to the interface* — prevents peel failure.
- Together with cobalt, the R/H system can boost steel-cord pull-out force from ~500 N to **>800 N**.

---

#### **Solution to Q-B(b):**

| Parameter | Inner Liner Compound | Sidewall Compound | Justification (one line) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **(i) Base Elastomer** | BIIR (Bromobutyl) 100 phr | NR 50 + EPDM 30 + E-SBR 20 | BIIR for air impermeability; EPDM blend for ozone + flex resistance in sidewall |
| **(ii) Filler System** | N660 (GPF) 60 phr | N550 (FEF) 40 phr | Platy filler for liner; semi-reinforcing CB for sidewall weatherability |
| **(iii) Antidegradant** | TMQ 1.0 (mild) | 6PPD 3.0 + TMQ 1.5 + Wax 2.5 | Liner is shielded from light/ozone; sidewall is fully exposed → heavy antidegradant package |
| **(iv) Cure System** | ZnO 3 + Stearic 1 + Sulphur 0.5 (low) + Zn-diethyldithiocarbamate 1.5 | ZnO 4 + Stearic 2 + Sulphur 2.2 + CBS 1.0 + TBBS 0.5 | Liner cures via ZnO/ZnDDC (BIIR is sensitive to free sulphur); sidewall uses conventional sulphur cure for high fatigue life |

**Key Engineering Insight:**
- BIIR is a **saturated backbone** elastomer — free sulphur does not crosslink it well. Hence ZnO + ZnDDC is used to cure the butyl, and low sulphur is added *only* to co-cure with the carcass.
- Sidewall uses a **high-sulphur, semi-EV cure** (2.2 phr sulphur + CBS/TBBS) for maximum fatigue resistance.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls:**
> 1. Do NOT suggest **peroxide cure** for inner liner unless explicitly asked — it conflicts with BIIR's intended co-cure with NR carcass. Marks will be deducted.
> 2. Do NOT use **SBR** in sidewall without EPDM — pure SBR has poor ozone resistance and cracks within 6 months of outdoor exposure.
> 3. In steel-cord adhesion questions, **always mention BOTH cobalt AND R/H** — students often forget the resorcinol-hexamine part and lose 1–2 marks.
> 4. When asked "design a compound", always show the **total phr** at the end. A formulation without a total phr is incomplete.

---

## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

### A. The "Big Picture" (5 Bullet Revision)
- ✅ A modern radial tyre uses **8 to 12 distinct rubber compounds**, each tuned for one component.
- ✅ **End-use → functional demand → polymer choice** is the *golden chain* of tyre compound design.
- ✅ **S-SBR + silica + silane = tread for low RR + wet grip**; **NR + CB = carcass**; **BIIR = inner liner** — these are the three "default" choices.
- ✅ The **Magic Triangle** (Grip – RR – Wear) governs all trade-offs; optimization biases differ by tyre SKU.
- ✅ **Steel-cord adhesion** demands BOTH cobalt naphthenate AND resorcinol-hexamine in an NR-rich matrix.

### B. Component → Polymer One-Liner (MUST MEMORIZE)
| Component | Polymer |
|---|---|
| Tread Cap | S-SBR + NR |
| Tread Base | NR + BR |
| Sidewall | NR + EPDM + SBR |
| Inner Liner | BIIR (100%) |
| Carcass | NR (100%) |
| Bead Apex | NR + BR |
| Belt Skim | NR + Cobalt + R/H |
| Chafer | NR + SBR |

### C. The "phr" Golden Rule
> **Total phr ≈ 170–215** for almost every tyre compound. If your total is below 160, you are missing a major ingredient; if above 220, double-check.

### D. Five KTU "Must Mention" Items
1. **Cobalt naphthenate** (adhesion promoter).
2. **6PPD + Wax** (ozone protection package).
3. **Silane TESPT** (silica coupling — without it, silica does not perform).
4. **TDAE oil** (modern, REACH-compliant, replaces DAE since 2010).
5. **Green strength of NR** (the reason for 100% NR in carcass).

### E. Common Exam Traps
- ❌ SBR in **inner liner** (wrong — must be BIIR).
- ❌ Carbon black in **tread cap** of a green/eco tyre (wrong — must be silica).
- ❌ Low sulphur in **carcass** (wrong — high sulphur for fatigue).
- ❌ Missing **wax** in sidewall (wrong — wax is essential for ozone blooming).
- ❌ Forgetting **total phr** in the final answer.

### F. Standard of Formulation Validation
Always specify the **target properties** for any compound you design:
- Hardness (Shore A)
- Tensile Strength (MPa)
- Elongation at break (%)
- Mooney Viscosity ML(1+4)@100 °C
- Cure time t90 (min at 160 °C)
- DIN abrasion (mm³)
- Adhesion to substrate (N/cm or pull-out force in N)

> 🎯 **Final Word:** In the KTU 2024 ESE, the design question is usually worth 7–14 marks. Practice at least 3 full component formulations, 2 cost calculations, and 1 comparison table — these cover ~90% of the marks available on this topic.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPOT504 - TYRE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module II
> - **Topic:** Students can refer the appropriate code of practices. Students can fabricate different components of tyre with the help of suitable machineries. Assembling of tyres and curing of tyres should also be done according to the tyre type.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Codes of Practices in Tyre Manufacturing

### Formal Academic Definition
**Codes of Practice (CoP)** in the tyre industry refer to standardized technical documents issued by national and international regulatory bodies that prescribe the rules, specifications, dimensions, performance requirements, safety criteria, and testing methodologies for pneumatic tyres and their components. They function as the legally and commercially accepted "rulebook" governing raw material selection, geometric construction, manufacturing tolerances, curing parameters, marking conventions, and end-of-line quality verification.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024 Scheme: A student must recognize that **CoPs are NOT optional** — they are the binding link between *design intent* and *road-worthiness certification*. BIS, ISO, ECE-R30, ECE-R54, ECE-R75, DOT (FMVSS 109/119/139), ETRTO, TRA, JATMA, and GSO 150 are the most commonly referenced codes in Indian/Global tyre industries.

### Key Standards Organizations & Their Scope

| Body | Standard | Scope / Application |
|------|----------|---------------------|
| **BIS** (Bureau of Indian Standards) | IS 1566, IS 1567, IS 9070, IS 13498 | Mandatory in India for new pneumatic tyres |
| **ISO** (International Org. for Standardization) | ISO 4000-1, ISO 4209, ISO 4223-1 | Global dimensional & performance specs |
| **ECE** (UN Economic Commission for Europe) | ECE-R30 (Passenger), R54 (Commercial), R75 (MC), R106 (Agricultural) | Type-approval across Europe & signatory countries |
| **DOT** (US Dept. of Transportation) | FMVSS 109, 119, 139 | North American compliance |
| **ETRTO** (European Tyre & Rim Technical Org.) | ETRTO Yearbook | European dimensional standards |
| **TRA** (Tyre & Rim Association, USA) | TRA Yearbook | North American dimensional standards |
| **JATMA** (Japan Auto Tyre Mfrs. Assoc.) | JATMA Yearbook | Japanese standards |
| **GSO** (Gulf Standardization Org.) | GSO 150, GSO 151, GSO 1052 | GCC region compliance |

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Building Code"
> [!NOTE]
> **Think of CoPs as the "National Building Code" for tyres.** When an engineer constructs a high-rise, the Code dictates the concrete grade, steel reinforcement, fire-escape width, and maximum floor area — not because the architect is incapable, but because *public safety* demands uniformity and verifiability. Similarly, tyre CoPs dictate the minimum tread depth, ply rating, load index, speed symbol, and curing pressure because a tyre failure at 120 km/h is fatal. The **machinist's skill + the CoP's specification = a road-legal tyre**.

---

## 1.2 Tyre Component Fabrication — Component-wise Definition

A pneumatic tyre is **not a single molded object** — it is an engineered assembly of **6 to 30+ individual components**, each produced on dedicated machinery, then united in the building stage and chemically fused in the curing press.

### Master List of Tyre Components

| # | Component | Material | Function |
|---|-----------|----------|----------|
| 1 | **Tread** | Natural Rubber / SBR + Carbon Black | Road contact, wear resistance |
| 2 | **Sidewall** | NR + BR blend | Flex, weathering, branding |
| 3 | **Ply (Carcass)** | Rayon / Nylon / Polyester / Steel cord + rubber | Load-bearing, burst resistance |
| 4 | **Belt (Steel/Nylon/Aramid)** | Steel cord + rubber / Nylon / Aramid | Tread stability, puncture resistance |
| 5 | **Inner Liner** | Halobutyl rubber (IIR) | Air retention |
| 6 | **Bead Core** | High-tensile steel wire (0.95–1.83 mm) | Anchoring to rim |
| 7 | **Bead Filler (Apex)** | NR compound (hard, Shore A 80–95) | Bead stiffness & ride comfort |
| 8 | **Chafer Strip** | Nylon / Fabric-reinforced rubber | Rim chafe protection |
| 9 | **Cap Ply (JLB)** | Nylon overlay (mostly on PCR) | High-speed stability |
| 10 | **Toe / Gum Strip** | Unvulcanized rubber | Component bonding |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Question Trigger:** Examiners frequently ask *"List the components of a pneumatic tyre and state the material used."* Memorize the table above — it carries 5 marks on its own.

---

## 1.3 Tyre Assembly (Building) — Definition

**Tyre building** is the **layered, geometrically precise lamination** of all semi-finished components onto a rotating collapsible drum, in a specific order and angular orientation, such that the resulting "green (uncured) tyre" possesses the exact cross-sectional profile that will be inflated and vulcanized into the final shape.

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Tyre building is exactly like making a **multi-layer cake on a rolling pin** — each layer (ply, belt, sidewall, tread) is wound in turn, the pin is collapsed and removed, and the stack is then "baked" (cured) in a heated mold.

---

## 1.4 Tyre Curing (Vulcanization) — Definition

**Curing** is the controlled thermo-chemical process wherein the green tyre, placed inside a heated, profiled mold, is inflated by an internal elastomeric bladder and held at elevated temperature (typically **150–190 °C**) and pressure (typically **100–250 psi internal bladder pressure**) for a calculated dwell time, during which **sulphur crosslinks form between rubber polymer chains**, transforming the soft, plastic green tyre into a tough, elastic, dimensionally stable finished tyre with the moulded tread pattern.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Curing is *not* merely heating — it is the **simultaneous application of heat + pressure + time** that drives the **crosslinking reaction**, a process discovered by Charles Goodyear in 1839.

---

## 1.5 Tyre Type Classification (Curing & Assembly Dependence)

The curing/assembly method differs markedly with tyre category:

| Tyre Type | Category Symbol | Cure Method | Build Stage |
|-----------|-----------------|-------------|-------------|
| **Passenger Car Radial (PCR)** | R | Bag-O-Matic (BOM) press | 1st + 2nd stage |
| **Light Truck Radial (LTR)** | LT | BOM press, higher tonnage | 1st + 2nd stage |
| **Truck & Bus Radial (TBR)** | C/R | Large BOM or segmented mold | 1st + 2nd stage |
| **Off-the-Road (OTR)** | E | Multi-day, low-temp cure | 1st stage only (sometimes) |
| **Agricultural** | A | Low pressure, large volume | Single stage (often) |
| **Motorcycle (2-wheeler)** | MC | Auto-form or BOM | Single or 2-stage |
| **Bias (Cross-ply)** | B/D | Lower pressure cure | Single stage |
| **Solid Industrial** | — | Compression mold, no bladder | Compression press |
| **Aircraft** | — | Long cure, autoclave / sectional mold | Single stage |
| **Run-Flat / Self-Sealing** | RF | Modified cure cycle | 1st + 2nd stage |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Codes of Practice — Deep Dive

### 2.1.1 Why are CoPs Mandatory?

A tyre failure at highway speed is a **multi-fatality event**. The CoP enforces:

1. **Geometric uniformity** → interchangeability of tyres on rims.
2. **Load-bearing verification** → every tyre must carry its stated load at its stated speed.
3. **Material traceability** → DOT serial code, BIS license number, ECE approval mark.
4. **Performance benchmark** → high-speed endurance, rolling resistance, wet grip, noise.
5. **Ageing & endurance** → heat-resistance and inflation-pressure-loss tests.

### 2.1.2 Sidewall Markings Decoded (ISO & ECE)

Example: **205/55 R 16 91 V**
- **205** = Section width in **mm**
- **55** = Aspect ratio (sidewall height = 55 % of width)
- **R** = Radial construction
- **16** = Rim diameter in **inches**
- **91** = Load index (91 = **615 kg**)
- **V** = Speed symbol (V = up to **240 km/h**)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Sidewall markings are like the **Aadhar card** of a tyre — they reveal its identity, capability, and origin in a glance.

### 2.1.3 DOT Date Code

The last 4 digits of the DOT serial number indicate the manufacture week and year (e.g., **2318** = 23rd week of 2018). For KTU, recall that tyres older than 5–6 years (per OEM guidance) are typically NOT recommended for service.

---

## 2.2 Component Fabrication — Machine-wise Theory

### 2.2.1 Compounding & Mixing Machines

#### (a) **Banbury Internal Mixer (Farrel Bridgewater)**
- Invented: 1916, by Fernley H. Banbury.
- Function: Intensive batch mixing of rubber + fillers + curatives.
- Operating parameters:
  - Rotor speed: **30–80 rpm** (variable)
  - Chamber volume: **25 L to 650 L** depending on model
  - Tip speed: **20–35 m/s**
  - Batch mass: ~**0.55 × chamber volume × bulk density**
- Sequence: **Polymer → carbon black → oil → zinc oxide → accelerator → sulphur (LAST)**.
- Why sulphur last? — Premature vulcanization ("scorch") must be avoided.

#### (b) **Two-Roll Mill (Open Mill)**
- Function: Final blending, warming, sheet-out, additive incorporation.
- Roll sizes: typically 600 mm × 1500 mm (diameter × length).
- Friction ratio: **1 : 1.1 to 1 : 1.4** (front roll slower than back).
- Operating temperature: **40–80 °C**.

### 2.2.2 Calendering Machine (for Ply & Belt Production)

**Definition:** A calender is a precision 4-roll or Z-roll system that coats fabric/cord with a thin rubber skim coat under controlled nip pressure, producing a continuous sheet that is then bias-cut into plies.

- **Roll configurations**: I, L, Z, S, inverted-L.
- **Nip pressure**: **20–80 bar**.
- **Line speed**: **10–90 m/min**.
- **Coating thickness**: **0.3–1.5 mm**.
- **Cord tension**: maintained constant via dancer rolls.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why calendering?** Rubber cannot penetrate textile or steel cords by simple dipping. The calender's high nip pressure forces the rubber compound *into* the cord interstices, giving the **adhesion strength** needed to resist centrifugal force at speed.

### 2.2.3 Extrusion Machines

#### (a) **Tread Extruder**
- Output: continuous tread profile with side wings.
- Die: pre-shaped to match the tread contour.
- **Vacuum extruder** is preferred to remove trapped air (porosity elimination).
- Throughput: **2–20 m/min**, depending on size.

#### (b) **Inner Liner Extruder**
- Extrudes a **butyl rubber sheet** (typically 1.0–2.5 mm thick).
- Width = circumference of the largest tyre to be built on the line.
- Special care: low contamination, surface smoothness (becomes air-retaining layer).

#### (c) **Sidewall Extruder**
- Extrudes sidewall rubber with embedded branding, whitewall strips, or rib profiles.

#### (d) **Apex (Bead Filler) Extruder**
- Extrudes triangular cross-section hard rubber.
- Stiffer Shore A (typically **80–95**).

### 2.2.4 Bead Making Machines

A **bead** is the inextensible anchor of the tyre — it must hold the inflated tyre on the rim under all loads.

- **Bead Winder**: winds multiple strands (typically 5–35 strands) of brass/bronze-coated high-tensile steel wire into a helical ring.
  - Wire diameter: **0.95 mm to 1.83 mm**.
  - Number of wires per bead: **5 to 35**.
  - Winding pitch: precise per bead size.
- **Bead Wrapping Machine (Rubber Coater)**: extrudes a rubber cover around the wound wire bundle.
- **Bead Cutting & Splicing Machine**: cuts the rubber-covered ring to the desired circumference and splices the ends with a **45° scarf joint** under high pressure for full strength.

### 2.2.5 Component Cutting Machines

- **Belt Cutter (45°/90° cutter)**: precision cuts steel-belt strips and nylon overlay strips at the engineered angle.
- **Ply Cutter (Bias Cutter)**: cuts calendered cord-rubber sheets at the engineered bias angle (e.g., **90° for radial carcass, ~55–60° for bias carcass**).
- **Chafer Strip Cutter**: cuts narrow fabric-reinforced strips.

---

## 2.3 Tyre Building (Assembly) — Deep Theory

### 2.3.1 First-Stage Building (Drum Building)

For **radial tyres**, building is split into two stages.

**Stage 1** (Flat Drum):
1. Drum rotates.
2. Inner liner is wound on first.
3. Ply/carcass layers are applied (1 or 2 plies for PCR; 1–3 for TBR).
4. Beads are placed (with apex).
5. Sidewalls are applied.
6. First-stage belts (sometimes) — no, belts typically go in Stage 2.
7. Drum is collapsed and removed → **Stage-1 "green tyre"** is a flat, cylindrical, ring-stacked blank.

### 2.3.2 Second-Stage Building (Shape/Expand Drum)

**Stage 2** (Expanding Drum):
1. Stage-1 blank is loaded.
2. Belt package is applied (1, 2, or 3 steel belts + optional cap ply / JLB).
3. Tread + gum strip applied.
4. Drum expands radially — the flat blank is **shaped** into a torus profile.
5. Stage-2 green tyre is removed and stored for curing.

### 2.3.3 Bias Tyre Building

Bias tyres use **single-stage building** because no second expansion is needed:
- Ply layers wound sequentially (4, 6, 8 … depending on rating).
- Beads placed, sidewalls applied, chafer added, tread wound last.
- Drum removed → green tyre is essentially ready for curing.

---

## 2.4 Tyre Curing (Vulcanization) — Deep Theory

### 2.4.1 The Vulcanization Reaction

Sulphur crosslinking (simplified):

$$
\text{R–S–S–S–R} \;+\; \text{R'–H} \;\xrightarrow{\text{Heat + Accelerator}}\; \text{R–S}_x\text{–R'} \;+\; \text{H}_2\text{S} \uparrow
$$

In modern industry, **accelerators** (e.g., MBT, CBS, TBBS, DPG) and **activators** (ZnO + stearic acid) drastically reduce cure time and temperature while improving crosslink density and ageing resistance.

### 2.4.2 The Three Pillars of Cure

| Pillar | Typical Value for PCR | Typical Value for TBR |
|--------|-----------------------|------------------------|
| **Temperature** | 165–180 °C | 150–170 °C |
| **Pressure (internal bladder)** | 100–180 psi (≈ 7–12 bar) | 150–250 psi (≈ 10–17 bar) |
| **Time** | 10–18 minutes | 30–90 minutes |

### 2.4.3 Cure Time — Calculation via Arrhenius Equation

$$
t_{90} \;=\; A \cdot e^{\dfrac{E_a}{R T}}
$$

Where:
- $t_{90}$ = time to reach 90 % cure (minutes)
- $A$ = pre-exponential constant (compound-specific)
- $E_a$ = activation energy (~**80–100 kJ/mol** for typical NR/SBR systems)
- $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = absolute temperature (K)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Catch:** Many students write *"Arrhenius says higher T means higher rate, so cure is faster."* The correct formulation is $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$ where $k$ is the rate constant. **Cure time $t_{90} \propto 1/k$**, so **higher T ⇒ lower $t_{90}$ ⇒ faster cure.**

### 2.4.4 Equivalent Cure Time (ECT) — Variable Temperature Adjustment

If mould temperature is not constant, we compute the equivalent cure time at a reference temperature $T_{ref}$:

$$
t_{eq} \;=\; \int_{0}^{t_{final}} e^{\left[ \dfrac{E_a}{R}\left( \dfrac{1}{T_{ref}} - \dfrac{1}{T(t)} \right) \right]} \, dt
$$

For a stepped temperature profile (e.g., steam-on, then plateau):

$$
t_{eq} \;=\; \sum_{i} \Delta t_i \cdot \exp\left[ \dfrac{E_a}{R}\left( \dfrac{1}{T_{ref}} - \dfrac{1}{T_i} \right) \right]
$$

### 2.4.5 Cure Press Tonnage

The press must apply sufficient **clamping force** to overcome the internal bladder pressure projected on the mould surface, plus a small margin for mould seal:

$$
F_{press} \;=\; P_{bladder} \times A_{mould} \times k_{safety}
$$

Where:
- $F_{press}$ = press clamping force (**kN** or **Tons**)
- $P_{bladder}$ = bladder inflation pressure (**MPa**)
- $A_{mould}$ = projected tread area (**m²**)
- $k_{safety}$ = safety factor = **1.05 to 1.15**

### 2.4.6 Bag-O-Matic (BOM) Press — Working Principle

The BOM press (invented by Goodyear in the 1950s) features:
- Upper and lower mould halves (steam heated).
- A central **bladder** (silicone or butyl rubber) mounted on a lower pot.
- Loading chuck descends, places the green tyre onto the bladder.
- Mould closes; bladder is inflated with hot water / steam / inert gas.
- Curing proceeds; bladder deflates; mould opens; cured tyre is ejected by a stripper.

### 2.4.7 Segmented Mould Press (for OTR / Giant Tyres)

For tyres > 57" rim diameter, the mould is built in **multiple sectors** that close radially (segmented mould) instead of vertically. This allows mould masses of **40–120 tonnes** to be opened with reasonable tonnage requirements.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations, Process Flows & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Cure Time from Arrhenius Equation

> **Problem Statement:** A tyre compound has $E_a = 90 \,\text{kJ/mol}$ and a reference $t_{90,ref} = 12$ minutes at $T_{ref} = 433 \,\text{K}$ (i.e., 160 °C). The plant runs at $T = 453 \,\text{K}$ (180 °C). Estimate the new cure time.

### Step 1 — Recall Arrhenius Form
The rate constant $k$ follows:

$$
k \;=\; A \cdot e^{-E_a / (R T)}
$$

### Step 2 — Cure Time Inversely Proportional to Rate

Since $t_{90} \propto 1/k$, we have:

$$
t_{90} \;\propto\; e^{+E_a / (R T)}
$$

### Step 3 — Form the Ratio

$$
\dfrac{t_{90,\, new}}{t_{90,\, ref}} \;=\; \dfrac{e^{+E_a / (R T_{new})}}{e^{+E_a / (R T_{ref})}} \;=\; \exp\!\left[ \dfrac{E_a}{R}\left( \dfrac{1}{T_{new}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{ref}} \right) \right]
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute Values

Plug in $E_a = 90\,000 \,\text{J/mol}$, $R = 8.314 \,\text{J/(mol·K)}$, $T_{new} = 453 \,\text{K}$, $T_{ref} = 433 \,\text{K}$.

Compute the bracket term:

$$
\dfrac{1}{T_{new}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{ref}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{453} - \dfrac{1}{433}
$$

$$
\dfrac{1}{453} \;\approx\; 2.2075 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{K}^{-1}
$$

$$
\dfrac{1}{433} \;\approx\; 2.3095 \times 10^{-3} \,\text{K}^{-1}
$$

$$
\text{Difference} \;=\; (2.2075 - 2.3095) \times 10^{-3} \;=\; -1.020 \times 10^{-4} \,\text{K}^{-1}
$$

### Step 5 — Multiply by $E_a / R$

$$
\dfrac{E_a}{R} \cdot (-1.020 \times 10^{-4}) \;=\; \dfrac{90\,000}{8.314} \cdot (-1.020 \times 10^{-4})
$$

$$
\dfrac{90\,000}{8.314} \;\approx\; 10\,825.6 \,\text{K}
$$

$$
10\,825.6 \cdot (-1.020 \times 10^{-4}) \;\approx\; -1.1042
$$

### Step 6 — Take the Exponential

$$
\dfrac{t_{90,\,new}}{t_{90,\,ref}} \;=\; e^{-1.1042} \;\approx\; 0.331
$$

### Step 7 — Solve for $t_{90,\,new}$

$$
t_{90,\,new} \;=\; 0.331 \times 12 \,\text{min} \;\approx\; 3.97 \,\text{min} \;\approx\; 4 \,\text{minutes}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** A 20 K temperature rise **cuts cure time to one-third**. This is why *process engineers* must tightly control platen temperature — a +5 K drift can scorch (over-cure); a −5 K drift leaves the tyre under-cured.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Press Clamping Tonnage

> **Problem Statement:** A PCR passenger tyre requires $P_{bladder} = 12 \,\text{bar} = 1.2 \,\text{MPa}$. The mould projected tread area is $0.07 \,\text{m}^2$. Use $k_{safety} = 1.1$. Find press tonnage.

### Step 1 — State Formula

$$
F_{press} \;=\; P_{bladder} \cdot A_{mould} \cdot k_{safety}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute

$$
F_{press} \;=\; (1.2 \times 10^{6} \,\text{Pa}) \cdot (0.07 \,\text{m}^2) \cdot (1.1)
$$

### Step 3 — Multiply

$$
F_{press} \;=\; 1.2 \times 10^{6} \times 0.07 \times 1.1
$$

$$
1.2 \times 10^{6} \times 0.07 \;=\; 8.4 \times 10^{4} \,\text{N}
$$

$$
8.4 \times 10^{4} \times 1.1 \;=\; 9.24 \times 10^{4} \,\text{N}
$$

### Step 4 — Convert to Tonnes

$$
F_{press} \;=\; \dfrac{92\,400 \,\text{N}}{9.81 \times 10^{3} \,\text{N/ton}} \;\approx\; 9.42 \,\text{tons}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality:** A small PCR press is rated **~10–30 tons**; a TBR press is **~150–300 tons**; a giant OTR press reaches **~4,000+ tons**. Apollo, MRF, and Ceat presses in India range from **80 tons to 2,500 tons**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sidewall Height from Marking

> **Problem Statement:** A tyre is marked **225/65 R 17**. Find section width $W$, sidewall height $H$, and overall diameter.

### Step 1 — Width

$$
W \;=\; 225 \,\text{mm}
$$

### Step 2 — Sidewall Height (Aspect Ratio)

$$
H \;=\; 0.65 \times 225 \;=\; 146.25 \,\text{mm}
$$

### Step 3 — Rim Diameter in mm

$$
D_{rim} \;=\; 17 \,\text{inch} \times 25.4 \,\frac{\text{mm}}{\text{inch}} \;=\; 431.8 \,\text{mm}
$$

### Step 4 — Overall Diameter

$$
D_{overall} \;=\; D_{rim} + 2 H
$$

$$
D_{overall} \;=\; 431.8 + 2 \times 146.25
$$

$$
D_{overall} \;=\; 431.8 + 292.5 \;=\; 724.3 \,\text{mm}
$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Bead Wire Load Capacity

> **Problem Statement:** A 16" bead uses 24 wires, each diameter $d = 1.27$ mm, ultimate tensile strength $UTS = 2200$ MPa. Find the static bead wire load capacity (use factor of safety = 3).

### Step 1 — Area of One Wire

$$
A_{wire} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{4} d^2 \;=\; \dfrac{\pi}{4} (1.27 \times 10^{-3})^2 \,\text{m}^2
$$

$$
A_{wire} \;=\; 0.7854 \times 1.6129 \times 10^{-6} \;\approx\; 1.267 \times 10^{-6} \,\text{m}^2
$$

### Step 2 — Ultimate Load per Wire

$$
F_{ult,1} \;=\; UTS \times A_{wire} \;=\; 2200 \times 10^{6} \times 1.267 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
F_{ult,1} \;\approx\; 2787 \,\text{N} \;\approx\; 2.79 \,\text{kN}
$$

### Step 3 — All Wires in the Bead

$$
F_{ult,bead} \;=\; n \times F_{ult,1} \;=\; 24 \times 2.79 \;=\; 66.96 \,\text{kN}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply Factor of Safety

$$
F_{allowable} \;=\; \dfrac{F_{ult,bead}}{FoS} \;=\; \dfrac{66.96}{3} \;\approx\; 22.32 \,\text{kN}
$$

---

## 3.5 Process Flow — Sequential Tyre Manufacturing Pipeline

The complete manufacturing pipeline for a **Passenger Car Radial (PCR)** tyre is:

1. **Compounding** → Banbury mixer → Masterbatch.
2. **Calendering** → Ply sheets and steel belt strips.
3. **Extrusion** → Tread, sidewall, inner liner, apex.
4. **Bead making** → Winding, wrapping, cutting, splicing.
5. **Component cutting** → Ply, belt, chafer at correct angles.
6. **Stage-1 Building** → Inner liner + ply + bead + sidewall.
7. **Stage-2 Building** → Belt + tread application on expanding drum.
8. **Curing** → BOM press, 165–180 °C, 10–18 min.
9. **Post-Cure Inflation** → Inflates to design OD to set shape.
10. **Inspection & Marking** → Uniformity, balance, sidewall branding.
11. **Storage & Dispatch** → Warehousing.

---

|-----------|-----------------|----------------------|----------------------------|
| Bead | Winder + Coater + Cutter | TRA / ETRTO bead seat | Wire count, splice strength |
| Inner Liner | Cold-feed extruder | ISO 4223-1 | Halobutyl purity, thickness |

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any six components of a pneumatic tyre and state the material used for each.**

#### Model Answer:

1. **Tread** – Natural Rubber / SBR blend with carbon black and silica.
2. **Carcass (Ply)** – Polyester / Rayon / Nylon / Steel cords coated with skim rubber.
3. **Belt (Steel belt for radial)** – Steel cord + rubber skim.
4. **Inner Liner** – Halobutyl (IIR) rubber, ~1–2 mm thick.
5. **Sidewall** – NR + BR blend with anti-ozonant waxes.
6. **Bead** – Brass-coated high-tensile steel wires wrapped in rubber.
7. **Bead Filler (Apex)** – Hard natural rubber compound (Shore A 80–95).

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**What is the role of BIS and ECE-R30 codes in tyre manufacturing?**

#### Model Answer:

- **BIS (Bureau of Indian Standards)** issues standards like IS 1566, IS 1567, IS 9070, and IS 13498. These are **mandatory** for sale in India — covering dimensions, load index, speed symbol, performance, marking, and testing.
- **ECE-R30** is a UN regulation for **passenger car tyres**, addressing type-approval, marking (E-mark), dimensional conformity, load/speed endurance, and high-speed performance.
- Both ensure **interchangeability, road safety, and legal marketability** of tyres; manufacturers must test and certify every design against these codes before commercial release.

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks)

### `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**Question A(a) [7 Marks]** — Explain the step-by-step procedure for **Stage-1 and Stage-2 building of a radial passenger car tyre (PCR)** with a neat sketch and the order of component application.

#### Model Answer:

**Stage-1 Building (Flat Drum):**

1. The **flat cylindrical drum** of suitable bead-seat diameter is mounted on the Stage-1 builder.
2. **Inner liner** (halobutyl rubber sheet) is wound first — it forms the air-retaining innermost layer.
3. **First carcass ply** (polyester cord + skim rubber) is applied at 90° to the drum axis, with both ends spliced at the centreline.
4. (Optional) **Second carcass ply** is applied for LT/TBR; not needed for PCR.
5. **Beads** (with apex already wrapped) are positioned on both sides; the bead seats lock them.
6. **Sidewalls** (extruded strips) are applied over the carcass turn-ups near the bead regions.
7. The drum is **collapsed** (mechanically) and the Stage-1 cylindrical blank is removed.
8. Stage-1 blank is **transferred** to the Stage-2 builder.

**Stage-2 Building (Expanding Drum):**

1. Stage-1 blank is loaded onto the Stage-2 expanding drum.
2. **First steel belt** is applied at a low angle (~18–22°) with cut-edge centred.
3. **Second steel belt** is applied at a higher angle (~22–26°) over the first.
4. (Optional) **Cap ply (JLB — Jointless Band of Nylon)** is overlaid for high-speed stability.
5. **Tread and gum strip** are applied as the outermost layer.
6. The drum is **expanded radially** — the flat blank is reshaped into a **torus profile**.
7. The green tyre is removed, stored briefly, and sent to the curing press.

> **Order summary:** Inner Liner → Ply 1 → (Ply 2) → Beads + Apex → Sidewall → [Transfer] → Belt 1 → Belt 2 → Cap Ply → Tread → Expand → Green Tyre.

---

**Question A(b) [7 Marks]** — Describe the **Bag-O-Matic (BOM) curing process** with a labelled schematic and state the typical temperature, pressure, and time range.

#### Model Answer:

**Schematic (refer Section 4.2 above):**

A BOM press consists of:
- **Upper steam-heated mould half**
- **Lower steam-heated mould half**
- **Central elastomeric bladder** mounted on a hollow pot
- **Loading chuck** for green tyre placement
- **Stripper mechanism** for ejecting cured tyre
- **PLC control** for time, temperature, and pressure profile

**Process Sequence:**

1. **Loading:** The loading chuck descends, picks up the green tyre, and places it onto the deflated bladder.
2. **Mould close:** Upper mould descends and clamps the green tyre between the two mould halves.
3. **Bladder inflation:** Hot water / steam at high pressure is introduced into the bladder, pressing the green tyre against the mould cavity.
4. **Cure phase:** Heat from the mould + internal pressure from the bladder are held for the cure duration.
5. **Pressure release:** Bladder is deflated.
6. **Mould open & eject:** Upper mould retracts; stripper pushes the cured tyre off the bladder.

**Typical Operating Parameters:**

| Parameter | PCR | TBR |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| Mould Temperature | **165–180 °C** | **150–170 °C** |
| Bladder Pressure | **100–180 psi (~7–12 bar)** | **150–250 psi (~10–17 bar)** |
| Cure Time | **10–18 minutes** | **30–90 minutes** |

**Crosslinking occurs** during the cure plateau, transforming the soft green tyre into a tough, elastic finished tyre with moulded tread pattern.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks)

### `[KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2023]`

**Question B(a) [7 Marks]** — Discuss the **different codes of practice** (national and international) governing tyre manufacturing. Explain their significance.

#### Model Answer:

**National Codes:**

1. **BIS (India)** — IS 1566 (car), IS 9070 (truck/bus), IS 1567, IS 13498 (motorcycle). These are mandatory for Indian market sale and cover dimensional, performance, and endurance criteria.
2. **DOT / FMVSS (USA)** — FMVSS 109 (passenger), 119 (commercial), 139 (new light truck). The DOT serial on the sidewall certifies compliance.
3. **GCC / GSO (Gulf countries)** — GSO 150 series for type-approval in the Gulf region.

**International / Regional Codes:**

4. **ECE Regulations (UN)** — ECE-R30 (PC), R54 (truck/bus), R75 (motorcycle), R106 (agricultural), R108 (retreaded), R117 (noise/wet grip/rolling resistance).
5. **ETRTO (Europe)** — Yearbook of dimensional specs for rims and tyres — interchangeable compatibility.
6. **TRA (USA)** — Yearbook of standard rim contours and inflation tables.
7. **JATMA (Japan)** — Yearbook of Japanese dimensional standards.
8. **ISO** — Global standards (ISO 4000-1, ISO 4209 series) for laboratory test methods.

**Significance:**

- **Safety:** Sets minimum performance under load, speed, heat, and ageing.
- **Interchangeability:** Defines exact rim fitment — no ambiguity between rim and tyre.
- **Trade facilitation:** A single ECE approval is accepted by 50+ countries.
- **Traceability:** DOT and ECE markings enable end-to-end accountability.
- **Liability:** Manufacturers are legally bound by these codes; non-compliance = market recall.

---

**Question B(b) [7 Marks]** — Compare **single-stage building (bias tyres)** vs **two-stage building (radial tyres)**. List the curing parameter differences for PCR vs OTR tyres.

#### Model Answer:

**Comparison: Single-Stage (Bias) vs Two-Stage (Radial)**

| Feature | Single-Stage (Bias) | Two-Stage (Radial) |
|---------|---------------------|---------------------|
| **Number of Drums** | One collapsible drum | Stage-1 flat drum + Stage-2 expanding drum |
| **Drum Shape** | Constant profile | Stage-1 cylindrical → Stage-2 torus |
| **Components Wound** | Ply, bead, sidewall, chafer, tread — all on one drum | Stage-1: liner, ply, bead, sidewall; Stage-2: belt, tread |
| **Operator Skill** | Higher (single drum, more layers) | Distributed across two stations |
| **Cycle Time** | 20–40 min | 5–10 min (Stage-1) + 3–5 min (Stage-2) |
| **Tyre Type** | Bias-ply (B/D), motorcycle, agricultural | Radial (R) — PCR, LTR, TBR |
| **Machines** | Single-stage builder (horizontal) | Stage-1 + Stage-2 builders, with transfer conveyor |
| **Belt Application** | Not applicable | Critical — done in Stage-2 at exact angles |

**Curing Parameter Differences: PCR vs OTR**

| Parameter | PCR | OTR |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| Tyre Size (Rim) | 13"–20" | 25"–51"+ |
| Mould Temperature | 165–180 °C | 130–150 °C (lower to avoid reversion in massive rubber mass) |
| Bladder Pressure | 7–12 bar | 25–40 bar (or higher; uses segment mould) |
| Cure Time | 10–18 min | 8–24 **hours** (some even 30+ hours) |
| Press Tonnage | 100–250 tons | 1,500–4,500 tons |
| Mould Type | BOM press | Segmented / collapsible mould |
| Platen Heat-up | Steam or electric | Steam (high capacity boiler) |
| Post-Cure Inflation | Optional / short | Often extended, several hours |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often forget to mention that **OTR curing uses lower temperature but much longer time**. The reason is to **avoid thermal reversion** — at very high temperature, thick rubber sections over-cure and become brittle. Hence, *low T, long t* is the OTR strategy.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in this Topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing BIS with ISO** — BIS is **mandatory national**, ISO is **voluntary international**. Examiner deducts ½ mark if you treat them as equivalent.
> 2. **Forgetting to mention 'sulphur crosslinking'** in the curing answer — without the crosslinking reaction, curing is just "heating"; mention **accelerators + activators (ZnO/stearic acid)** for full marks.
> 3. **Missing units** in numerical problems — e.g., ECT formula needs **absolute temperature in K**, not °C. Many students lose 1–2 marks here.
> 4. **Wrong stage sequence** in tyre building — **Inner liner is FIRST** (PCR/TBR radial). Many students start with "ply" which loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Forgetting safety factor** in press tonnage calculation — typical answer expects $k_{safety} = 1.1$ to be shown.
> 6. **Mis-stating cure temperature** — for OTR, students often write "180 °C like PCR" — wrong, OTR uses **130–150 °C**.
> 7. **Ignoring cap ply (JLB)** — modern high-speed PCR has a nylon overlay called JLB; missing this costs a mark.
> 8. **Component names** — "Carcass" and "Ply" are sometimes used interchangeably, but **Carcass = assembly of all plies**; **Ply = single layer**.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Save this for the night before the exam):**

- [ ] **Codes of Practice** = BIS (mandatory India), ECE-R30/54/75/106, DOT FMVSS 109/119/139, ISO 4000-1, ETRTO, TRA, JATMA, GSO.
- [ ] **Tyre marking** 205/55 R 16 91 V → width 205 mm, aspect 55 %, Radial, 16" rim, load index 91 (615 kg), speed V (240 km/h).
- [ ] **Components (in build order for PCR):** Inner Liner → Ply → Beads + Apex → Sidewall → Belt 1 → Belt 2 → Cap Ply (JLB) → Tread.
- [ ] **Inner Liner material** = Halobutyl (IIR) — air retention.
- [ ] **Bead material** = brass-coated high-tensile steel wire (0.95–1.83 mm).
- [ ] **Banbury mixer** = intensive batch mixer, sulphur added LAST.
- [ ] **Calender** = coats cord fabric with rubber skim; 4-roll, Z-roll common.
- [ ] **Extruders** = for tread, sidewall, inner liner, apex.
- [ ] **Two-stage building** = Stage-1 flat drum + Stage-2 expanding drum; used for radials.
- [ ] **Single-stage building** = bias tyres, motorcycle, agricultural.
- [ ] **Curing = heat + pressure + time + crosslinking** (sulphur + accelerator).
- [ ] **Cure temperature** = PCR 165–180 °C; OTR 130–150 °C; TBR 150–170 °C.
- [ ] **Cure time** = PCR 10–18 min; TBR 30–90 min; OTR 8–24 h.
- [ ] **Arrhenius formula:** $k = A e^{-E_a/RT}$; $E_a$ for NR/SBR ≈ 80–100 kJ/mol; $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K).
- [ ] **Press tonnage** = $F = P \times A \times k_{safety}$; PCR ~10–30 tons, TBR ~150–300 tons, OTR ~4,000 tons.
- [ ] **Bag-O-Matic press** = most common cure press; uses internal elastomeric bladder.
- [ ] **Post-cure inflation** = sets final shape; ~30–60 s holding at low pressure.
- [ ] **DOT date code** = last 4 digits = week + year of manufacture.
- [ ] **ECE mark** = "E" followed by a number indicating the issuing country.
- [ ] **Green tyre** = uncured assembled tyre; **Cured tyre** = finished vulcanized product.
- [ ] **Sidewall marking utility** = enables rim matching, load calculation, speed limit compliance, traceability.

---

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPOT504 - TYRE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module III
> - **Topic:** Any tyre testing methods can also be performed by the student on the tyre he/she has designed.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔬 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, this Module III topic bridges the gap between **Tyre Design** (theoretical) and **Tyre Performance Validation** (experimental). It is formally stated as:

> *"Students who have designed a tyre (covering dimensions, profile, ply rating, compound selection) shall perform standardised testing methods on the same design prototype to experimentally validate performance characteristics, ensuring that the designed parameters meet industry acceptance norms."*

In essence, the student transforms a **CAD/Bench-designed tyre** into a **physically testable artefact** and subjects it to:

- **Destructive Tests** (Plunger Test, Tensile Test, Abrasion Test)
- **Non-Destructive Tests** (Dimensional Check, Hardness, Static Load, Inflation Pressure Loss)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this topic matters in KTU 2024 Examinations:**
> This is an **integrative learning outcome** — questions here will test whether the student can **connect design parameters (from Module II) with testing outcomes (Module III)**. Examiners frequently frame this as *"Validate your design using the plunger test"* style problems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Final Exam for Your Designed Product"

Imagine you designed a car on paper — beautiful aerodynamics, perfect dimensions, stunning looks. But the **true test** is when you **manufacture a prototype** and crash-test it, brake-test it, and fuel-economy test it.

A tyre is the same:

- **Module I-II = Drawing the design** (tread pattern, ply arrangement, sidewall geometry, compound recipe)
- **Module III = Driving the design through tests** (does the plunger puncture it? how much energy can it absorb? how does it wear?)

Just as a student engineer wouldn't claim their bridge design is "safe" without load-testing it, a Tyre Technology student cannot claim their **designed tyre is roadworthy** without running **standardised tests**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standards Governing Tyre Testing (must be memorised for KTU):**
> - **BIS 1566 / IS 1566** — Indian Standard for Pneumatic Tyres (Passenger Car)
> - **ISO 10191** — Plunger Test method
> - **ASTM D412** — Tensile Testing of Vulcanised Rubber
> - **ASTM D2240** — Shore A Hardness
> - **ASTM D5963** — DIN Abrasion Test
> - **ISO 28580** — Laboratory Abrasion Test (LAT 100)

## 1.3 The Six Core Tests a Student Must Perform

| # | Test Name | Type | Key Output |
|---|-----------|------|-----------|
| 1 | Dimensional Measurement | Non-Destructive | Section width, OD, BSD |
| 2 | Hardness (Shore A / IRHD) | Non-Destructive | Hardness value (50–75 Shore A) |
| 3 | Static Load & Deflection | Non-Destructive | Loaded radius, deflection |
| 4 | Plunger (Breaking Energy) | Destructive | Energy in **Joules** |
| 5 | Tensile Test on Compound | Destructive | TS, EB, Modulus |
| 6 | Abrasion Resistance | Destructive | Volume loss in **mm³** |

Each test directly validates a **design decision** made earlier — for example, if you designed with high natural rubber content, your tensile test should show high elongation at break.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📐 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Theory: From Design to Validation

When a student designs a tyre (let's assume a **165/80 R13** passenger car radial for simplicity), they specify:
- Section width = 165 mm
- Aspect ratio = 80 → Section height = 132 mm
- Rim diameter = 13 inch
- Ply rating = 4
- Inflation pressure = 240 kPa
- Tread compound = SBR + BR + NR blend

The test methods then verify whether **each design choice** is physically realisable.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Each Test:**
> 1. **Dimensional Test** → Did our mould produce the right geometry?
> 2. **Hardness Test** → Is the rubber cured to the right durometer?
> 3. **Static Load Test** → Will the sidewall flex correctly under passenger load?
> 4. **Plunger Test** → Can the tyre survive a nail/sharp object penetration (a key safety metric)?
> 5. **Tensile Test** → Are the reinforcing plies and compound strong enough?
> 6. **Abrasion Test** → Will the tread last 40,000 km as designed?

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Test / Parameter | Governing Equation | Standard / Unit | Design Validation Purpose |
|---|------------------|-------------------|-----------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | **Tensile Strength (TS)** | $TS = \dfrac{F_{max}}{A_0}$ | MPa (N/mm²) | Validates ply + compound strength |
| 2 | **Elongation at Break (EB)** | $EB = \dfrac{L_{b} - L_0}{L_0} \times 100$ | **%** | Validates rubber elasticity |
| 3 | **Modulus at X% Elongation** | $M_{X} = \dfrac{F_{X}}{A_0}$ | MPa | Validates stiffness at design strain |
| 4 | **Shore A Hardness** | Empirical durometer reading | Shore A (0–100) | Validates cure state |
| 5 | **IRHD Hardness** | $IRHD \approx Shore A$ (within ±2) | IRHD | International equivalent of Shore A |
| 6 | **Static Deflection** | $\delta = R_{free} - R_{loaded}$ | **mm** | Validates sidewall compliance |
| 7 | **Deflection Ratio** | $DR = \dfrac{\delta}{R_{free}} \times 100$ | **%** (typical 7–12%) | Validates ride comfort vs handling |
| 8 | **Section Width Growth** | $\Delta SW = SW_{loaded} - SW_{free}$ | mm | Validates carcass rigidity |
| 9 | **Breaking Energy (Plunger)** | $E_b = \displaystyle\int_0^{h} F(h)\,dh$ | **Joules (J)** | Validates puncture resistance |
| 10 | **Approximate Breaking Energy (linear)** | $E_b \approx \dfrac{1}{2} F_{max} \cdot h_{max}$ | J | Quick KTU calculation |
| 11 | **Abrasion Volume Loss (DIN)** | $V_{loss} = \dfrac{m_1 - m_2}{\rho_{rubber}}$ | **mm³** | Validates tread wear life |
| 12 | **Abrasion Resistance Index (ARI)** | $ARI = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{V_{sample}} \times 100$ | **%** | Relative wear performance |
| 13 | **Recommended Inflation Pressure (Load)** | $P = \dfrac{W}{A_{contact} \cdot k}$ | kPa | Validates load-carrying design |
| 14 | **Air Retention (Pressure Loss)** | $\Delta P = \dfrac{P_0 - P_t}{t}$ | kPa/day | Validates inner liner design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Memory Aid:** 
> For the **Plunger Test**, the breaking energy is the **area under the Force–Penetration curve**. In exams, when only peak force $F_{max}$ and penetration $h$ are given, use the **triangular approximation**: $E_b \approx \frac{1}{2} F_{max} \cdot h$. For more accurate trapezoidal estimation, break the curve into segments.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **OEM Validation** — Every new tyre model from **MRF, Apollo, CEAT, Bridgestone** must pass plunger + endurance tests before homologation.
- **Material Substitution** — When a compounder replaces NR with synthetic polyisoprene, the tensile + abrasion tests are run to confirm equivalence.
- **Design Iteration** — If plunger test fails (low breaking energy), the designer increases **ply rating** or switches to a **higher-modulus belt steel**.
- **Warranty Justification** — ARI (Abrasion Resistance Index) values above 100 (vs reference) are used in marketing "100,000 km tread life" claims.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Framework — Mapping Design Choices to Tests

> [!NOTE]
> **The 3-Pillar Analytical Matrix used in KTU Valuation:**
> Every tyre test the student performs is interpreted through:
> 1. **DESIGN INPUT** → what the student specified
> 2. **TEST METHOD** → how it is measured
> 3. **PASS/FAIL CRITERION** → standard threshold

## 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Worked Example: PLUNGER (BREAKING ENERGY) TEST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the highest-weightage KTU problem type on this topic. Master it fully.**

### **Problem Statement**

A KTU student has designed a **145/80 R12** passenger car tyre. The tyre is subjected to a **Plunger Test** as per ISO 10191. The plunger has a **19 mm diameter hemispherical tip**. The force–penetration data recorded is:

| Penetration $h$ (mm) | Force $F$ (N) |
|:--------------------:|:-------------:|
| 0                    | 0             |
| 5                    | 1800          |
| 10                   | 3400          |
| 15                   | 4600          |
| 20                   | 5400          |
| 25                   | 5800          |
| 28                   | 5950 (peak)   |
| 30                   | 5500          |
| 32                   | 0 (rupture)   |

**Required:**
1. Plot the F-h curve conceptually and identify the rupture point.
2. Calculate the **Breaking Energy** using the **trapezoidal rule**.
3. State whether the tyre **passes** the BIS 1566 minimum of **≥ 250 J** for passenger tyres.

### **Step-by-Step Solution**

**Step 1 — Set up the trapezoidal integration:**

Breaking energy is the area under the F-h curve from h = 0 to h = h_rupture.

$$
E_b = \int_{0}^{h_{rupture}} F(h)\,dh \approx \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{(F_i + F_{i-1})}{2} \cdot (h_i - h_{i-1})
$$

**Step 2 — Compute each trapezoidal segment:**

> *Conversion logic: We treat each pair of consecutive data points as the corners of a trapezium, then sum all trapezium areas.*

Segment 1: $h = 0$ to $h = 5$ mm

$$
A_1 = \frac{(0 + 1800)}{2} \times (5 - 0) = 900 \times 5 = 4500 \text{ N·mm}
$$

Segment 2: $h = 5$ to $h = 10$ mm

$$
A_2 = \frac{(1800 + 3400)}{2} \times (10 - 5) = 2600 \times 5 = 13000 \text{ N·mm}
$$

Segment 3: $h = 10$ to $h = 15$ mm

$$
A_3 = \frac{(3400 + 4600)}{2} \times (15 - 10) = 4000 \times 5 = 20000 \text{ N·mm}
$$

Segment 4: $h = 15$ to $h = 20$ mm

$$
A_4 = \frac{(4600 + 5400)}{2} \times (20 - 15) = 5000 \times 5 = 25000 \text{ N·mm}
$$

Segment 5: $h = 20$ to $h = 25$ mm

$$
A_5 = \frac{(5400 + 5800)}{2} \times (25 - 20) = 5600 \times 5 = 28000 \text{ N·mm}
$$

Segment 6: $h = 25$ to $h = 28$ mm

$$
A_6 = \frac{(5800 + 5950)}{2} \times (28 - 25) = 5875 \times 3 = 17625 \text{ N·mm}
$$

Segment 7: $h = 28$ to $h = 30$ mm

$$
A_7 = \frac{(5950 + 5500)}{2} \times (30 - 28) = 5725 \times 2 = 11450 \text{ N·mm}
$$

Segment 8: $h = 30$ to $h = 32$ mm

$$
A_8 = \frac{(5500 + 0)}{2} \times (32 - 30) = 2750 \times 2 = 5500 \text{ N·mm}
$$

**Step 3 — Sum all segment areas (Total Breaking Energy in N·mm):**

$$
E_b^{Nmm} = 4500 + 13000 + 20000 + 25000 + 28000 + 17625 + 11450 + 5500
$$

$$
E_b^{Nmm} = 125075 \text{ N·mm}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert N·mm to Joules (1 J = 1000 N·mm):**

$$
E_b = \frac{125075}{1000} = 125.075 \text{ J}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with BIS 1566 Standard:**

| Parameter | Required (BIS 1566) | Obtained | Result |
|:---------:|:------------------:|:--------:|:------:|
| Breaking Energy | **≥ 250 J** | **125.075 J** | ❌ **FAIL** |

> [!WARNING]
> **Engineering Verdict:** The designed tyre **FAILS** the plunger test. The student must iterate the design — most likely by **increasing the ply rating (e.g., from 4 PR to 6 PR)**, **adding a steel belt**, or **using a higher-modulus tread compound**. This is a classic KTU "design-validation" outcome.

## 3.3 Second Worked Example — STATIC LOAD & DEFLECTION TEST

### **Problem Statement**

A student-designed **205/65 R15** tyre is subjected to a static load test. The recorded data:

- Free (unloaded) radius, $R_{free} = 315$ mm
- Loaded radius (under $W = 4500$ N at 220 kPa), $R_{loaded} = 290$ mm
- Free section width, $SW_{free} = 205$ mm
- Loaded section width, $SW_{loaded} = 216$ mm

**Required:**
1. Static deflection $\delta$
2. Deflection Ratio $DR$
3. Section Width Growth $\Delta SW$

### **Step-by-Step Solution**

**Step 1 — Static Deflection:**

$$
\delta = R_{free} - R_{loaded} = 315 - 290 = 25 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Deflection Ratio:**

$$
DR = \frac{\delta}{R_{free}} \times 100 = \frac{25}{315} \times 100
$$

$$
DR = 7.94 \text{ \%}
$$

**Step 3 — Section Width Growth:**

$$
\Delta SW = SW_{loaded} - SW_{free} = 216 - 205 = 11 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\Delta SW \text{ (\%)} = \frac{11}{205} \times 100 = 5.37 \text{ \%}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical KTU Acceptance Range:**
> - Deflection Ratio: **7% – 12%** → ✅ PASS (7.94% is within range)
> - Section Width Growth: **≤ 6%** → ✅ PASS (5.37% is acceptable)
> - A DR below 7% means the sidewall is **too stiff** (harsh ride); above 12% means **too soft** (poor handling).

## 3.4 Third Worked Example — TENSILE TEST ON DESIGNED COMPOUND

### **Problem Statement**

A student designs a **tread compound** for a truck tyre with the following test data:

- Specimen: **Dumbbell** shape, gauge length $L_0 = 25$ mm
- Cross-section at gauge: width $b = 6$ mm, thickness $t = 2.5$ mm
- Maximum force: $F_{max} = 285$ N
- Length at break: $L_b = 162.5$ mm
- Force at 100% elongation: $F_{100} = 42$ N
- Force at 300% elongation: $F_{300} = 156$ N

**Required:**
1. Tensile Strength
2. Elongation at Break
3. Modulus at 100% and 300%

### **Step-by-Step Solution**

**Step 1 — Original Cross-Sectional Area:**

$$
A_0 = b \times t = 6 \times 2.5 = 15 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Tensile Strength:**

$$
TS = \frac{F_{max}}{A_0} = \frac{285}{15} = 19.0 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Elongation at Break:**

$$
EB = \frac{L_b - L_0}{L_0} \times 100 = \frac{162.5 - 25}{25} \times 100
$$

$$
EB = \frac{137.5}{25} \times 100 = 550 \text{ \%}
$$

**Step 4 — Modulus at 100% Elongation:**

$$
M_{100} = \frac{F_{100}}{A_0} = \frac{42}{15} = 2.80 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Modulus at 300% Elongation:**

$$
M_{300} = \frac{F_{300}}{A_0} = \frac{156}{15} = 10.40 \text{ MPa}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Interpretation:** A truck tread compound should have:
> - TS ≥ 18 MPa → ✅ (19.0 MPa)
> - EB ≥ 500% → ✅ (550%)
> - M300 ≥ 8 MPa → ✅ (10.40 MPa)
> 
> The student's compound design is **validated** for truck application.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics
