<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]**

### **Q1. Define "Breaking Energy" in the context of a Plunger Test on a designed tyre. Mention the relevant Indian Standard. `[3 Marks]`**

**Model Answer:**

> Breaking Energy ($E_b$) is defined as the **total energy absorbed by a tyre** when a standardised hemispherical plunger (typically 19 mm diameter) is forced through the tread and into the carcass, measured as the **area under the Force–Penetration curve** up to the point of rupture. It is a direct measure of the **puncture resistance and structural integrity** of the tyre design.
>
> **Governing Standard:** **ISO 10191 / BIS 1566** (for pneumatic tyres).
>
> **Unit:** Joules (J)
>
> **Typical Acceptance Value for Passenger Car Tyre:** **≥ 250 J** (as per BIS 1566).

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

### **Q2. List any THREE standard tyre tests a student can perform on a designed tyre and state ONE key outcome of each. `[3 Marks]`**

**Model Answer:**

| # | Test | Standard | Key Outcome |
|---|------|:--------:|-------------|
| 1 | Plunger Test | ISO 10191 | Breaking Energy (J) — puncture resistance |
| 2 | Tensile Test on Tread Compound | ASTM D412 | Tensile Strength (MPa) — compound strength |
| 3 | Hardness Test | ASTM D2240 | Shore A value — cure state validation |
| 4 | DIN Abrasion Test | ASTM D5963 | Volume loss (mm³) — tread wear life |
| 5 | Static Load Test | BIS 1566 | Deflection ratio (%) — ride comfort |

*(Any three with outcome = full 3 marks)*

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students **commonly lose 3–4 marks** by writing only the procedure but **omitting the numerical analysis** (units, area under curve, percentage calculation). Always show the **final numerical answer with units** in a box.
> - Do **not confuse BIS 1566 minimum breaking energy values** for PC (≥ 250 J), LCV (≥ 400 J), and HCV (≥ 600 J) tyres — examiners frequently test this discrimination.
> - Always **mention the Standard number (e.g., ISO 10191, ASTM D412)** alongside each test description; omitting standards is a **2-mark deduction** as per KTU valuation key.
> - In Plunger Test, students often forget to **convert N·mm to Joules** (divide by 1000). This single error loses 2 marks.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) A student has designed a 165/80 R13 passenger car radial tyre. Describe in detail the Plunger Test procedure and the calculation methodology for Breaking Energy. State the BIS 1566 acceptance criterion.** `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**1. Plunger Test — Definition and Purpose (2 marks):**
The Plunger Test is a **destructive performance test** conducted on a manufactured tyre prototype to determine the **Breaking Energy** ($E_b$) — the energy required to force a hemispherical plunger through the tyre's tread, shoulder, and carcass. It simulates the worst-case puncture scenario.

**2. Apparatus Required (1 mark):**
- Hydraulic compression testing machine
- Hemispherical steel plunger, **19 mm diameter**
- Tyre mounting fixture (suitable for 13" rim)
- Pressure gauge, displacement sensor
- Data acquisition system (force vs. penetration)

**3. Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Mount the tyre on the specified rim and inflate to the recommended pressure (e.g., 240 kPa).
2. Condition the tyre at test temperature (25 ± 5 °C) for 3 hours.
3. Place the tyre on the test platform with the tread facing the plunger.
4. Position the plunger at the **centreline of the tread**.
5. Drive the plunger at a constant rate of **50 ± 5 mm/min** until complete rupture.
6. Record the **force–penetration data** continuously.

**4. Calculation Methodology (1.5 marks):**
Breaking Energy is calculated as the **area under the F-h curve** using the trapezoidal rule:

$$
E_b = \int_{0}^{h_r} F(h)\,dh \approx \sum \frac{(F_i + F_{i-1})}{2} \cdot \Delta h
$$

Convert N·mm to Joules: divide by 1000.

**5. BIS 1566 Acceptance Criterion (0.5 marks):**
For **165/80 R13 PC tyre**: **$E_b \geq 250$ J**.

---

#### **(b) During a Plunger Test on the above designed tyre, the following readings were recorded at a constant plunger speed of 50 mm/min:**

| Penetration h (mm) | 0 | 5 | 10 | 15 | 20 | 25 | 30 | 35 | 38 |
|:------------------:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|:-:|
| Force F (N)        | 0 | 1500 | 2900 | 4000 | 4800 | 5300 | 5400 | 4800 | 0 |

**Calculate the Breaking Energy using the trapezoidal rule. Does the tyre pass BIS 1566 standard (≥ 250 J)?** `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply trapezoidal rule (5 marks for full computation):**

> *Conversion logic: Each segment area = average of the two force values × interval width.*

Segment 1 (0 → 5 mm):

$$
A_1 = \frac{(0 + 1500)}{2} \times 5 = 3750 \text{ N·mm}
$$

Segment 2 (5 → 10 mm):

$$
A_2 = \frac{(1500 + 2900)}{2} \times 5 = 11000 \text{ N·mm}
$$

Segment 3 (10 → 15 mm):

$$
A_3 = \frac{(2900 + 4000)}{2} \times 5 = 17250 \text{ N·mm}
$$

Segment 4 (15 → 20 mm):

$$
A_4 = \frac{(4000 + 4800)}{2} \times 5 = 22000 \text{ N·mm}
$$

Segment 5 (20 → 25 mm):

$$
A_5 = \frac{(4800 + 5300)}{2} \times 5 = 25250 \text{ N·mm}
$$

Segment 6 (25 → 30 mm):

$$
A_6 = \frac{(5300 + 5400)}{2} \times 5 = 26750 \text{ N·mm}
$$

Segment 7 (30 → 35 mm):

$$
A_7 = \frac{(5400 + 4800)}{2} \times 5 = 25500 \text{ N·mm}
$$

Segment 8 (35 → 38 mm):

$$
A_8 = \frac{(4800 + 0)}{2} \times 3 = 7200 \text{ N·mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Sum (1 mark):**

$$
E_b = (3750 + 11000 + 17250 + 22000 + 25250 + 26750 + 25500 + 7200) \text{ N·mm}
$$

$$
E_b = 138700 \text{ N·mm} = 138.7 \text{ J}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare with standard (1 mark):**

| Required (BIS 1566) | Obtained | Result |
|:------------------:|:--------:|:------:|
| **≥ 250 J**        | **138.7 J** | ❌ **FAIL** |

**Conclusion:** The designed tyre **does NOT pass** the Plunger Test. The student must strengthen the design — likely by **increasing ply rating from 4 PR to 6 PR** or **adding a steel breaker belt**.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

#### **(a) Describe the Static Load Test and the Tensile Test procedures for a designed tyre. Mention the relevant standards and the typical acceptance values for a passenger car tyre.** `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**1. Static Load Test (3.5 marks):**
- **Standard:** BIS 1566 / IS 1566
- **Purpose:** Determines the loaded radius, section width under load, and deflection ratio — validates sidewall compliance.
- **Procedure:**
  1. Mount the tyre on the recommended rim and inflate to design pressure.
  2. Measure free radius ($R_{free}$) and free section width ($SW_{free}$).
  3. Apply the rated load ($W$) vertically through the spindle.
  4. After 5 min dwell, measure $R_{loaded}$ and $SW_{loaded}$.
  5. Calculate deflection $\delta = R_{free} - R_{loaded}$ and deflection ratio $DR = (\delta / R_{free}) \times 100$.
- **Acceptance:** DR between **7% – 12%** for PC tyres.

**2. Tensile Test (3.5 marks):**
- **Standard:** **ASTM D412**
- **Purpose:** Validates the **tensile strength, elongation at break, and modulus** of the rubber compound (typically the tread compound).
- **Procedure:**
  1. Cut a **dumbbell-shaped specimen** from the cured tread using Die C.
  2. Measure gauge length $L_0$ (typically 25 mm) and cross-section area $A_0$.
  3. Mount in a universal testing machine (Instron).
  4. Pull at **500 mm/min** until rupture.
  5. Record force and elongation continuously.
- **Acceptance for PC Tread Compound:**
  - Tensile Strength: **TS ≥ 17 MPa**
  - Elongation at Break: **EB ≥ 450 %**
  - Modulus at 300%: **M300 ≥ 8 MPa**

---

#### **(b) A student's designed tread compound was subjected to a Tensile Test (ASTM D412). Specimen cross-section: 4 mm × 2 mm. Gauge length: 20 mm. Results: F_max = 168 N, length at break = 130 mm, F at 100% = 32 N, F at 300% = 128 N. Calculate TS, EB, M100, and M300. State whether the compound is suitable for a PC tyre tread.** `[7 Marks]`

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area (0.5 marks):**

$$
A_0 = 4 \times 2 = 8 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Tensile Strength (1.5 marks):**

$$
TS = \frac{F_{max}}{A_0} = \frac{168}{8} = 21.0 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 3 — Elongation at Break (1.5 marks):**

$$
EB = \frac{130 - 20}{20} \times 100 = \frac{110}{20} \times 100 = 550 \text{ \%}
$$

**Step 4 — Modulus at 100% (1.5 marks):**

$$
M_{100} = \frac{F_{100}}{A_0} = \frac{32}{8} = 4.00 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 5 — Modulus at 300% (1.5 marks):**

$$
M_{300} = \frac{F_{300}}{A_0} = \frac{128}{8} = 16.00 \text{ MPa}
$$

**Step 6 — Verification (1 mark):**

| Parameter | PC Requirement | Obtained | Result |
|:---------:|:--------------:|:--------:|:------:|
| TS        | ≥ 17 MPa       | 21.0 MPa | ✅ PASS |
| EB        | ≥ 450 %        | 550 %    | ✅ PASS |
| M300      | ≥ 8 MPa        | 16.0 MPa | ✅ PASS |

**Conclusion:** The designed compound **PASSES** all three criteria and is **suitable for passenger car tyre tread application**.

---

## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module III Testing**

- ✅ **Plunger Test → Breaking Energy = Area under F-h curve** (use trapezoidal rule, convert N·mm → J by dividing by 1000)
- ✅ **BIS 1566 Breaking Energy minima:** PC tyre **≥ 250 J**, LCV **≥ 400 J**, HCV **≥ 600 J**, Two-wheeler **≥ 100 J**
- ✅ **Standard for Plunger Test:** ISO 10191; **Plunger diameter:** 19 mm hemispherical; **Plunger speed:** 50 mm/min
- ✅ **Tensile Test Standard:** ASTM D412, Die C specimen, **speed 500 mm/min**
- ✅ **PC tread compound criteria:** TS ≥ 17 MPa, EB ≥ 450%, M300 ≥ 8 MPa
- ✅ **Shore A Hardness Range for PC tread:** **55 – 70 Shore A**
- ✅ **Deflection Ratio Range:** **7% – 12%** for PC tyres (lower = harsh ride, higher = soft / unstable)
- ✅ **DIN Abrasion Test Standard:** ASTM D5963; **Volume loss** reported in **mm³**; **ARI ≥ 100** for good wear
- ✅ **Deflection formula:** $\delta = R_{free} - R_{loaded}$
- ✅ **Deflection Ratio formula:** $DR = (\delta / R_{free}) \times 100$
- ✅ **Tensile Strength formula:** $TS = F_{max} / A_0$
- ✅ **Elongation at Break formula:** $EB = [(L_b - L_0) / L_0] \times 100$
- ✅ **Modulus formula:** $M_X = F_X / A_0$
- ✅ **Breaking Energy (triangular approx.):** $E_b \approx \frac{1}{2} F_{max} \cdot h_{max}$
- ✅ **When plunger test FAILS →** Increase ply rating, add steel belt, or use higher-modulus compound
- ✅ **Section Width Growth typical:** ≤ 6% under rated load
- ✅ **Always quote the STANDARD alongside the test name** (KTU valuation: 2 marks per test for correct standard)
- ✅ **Hardness testers:** Shore A (analog, fast) vs IRHD (international standard, more accurate); values differ by ≤ 2 units in the 30–85 range
- ✅ **Air retention test:** Measure pressure drop in kPa/day; typical ≤ 5 kPa/day for tubeless PC tyres
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Students should submit the report to the faculty.
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBPOT504 - TYRE TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module Module IV
> - **Topic:** Students should submit the report to the faculty.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition (Module IV – Continuous Assessment Directive):**
> The directive *"Students should submit the report to the faculty"* is a formal **Continuous Internal Evaluation (CIE)** requirement specified under the KTU 2024 Scheme. In the context of **PBPOT504 – Tyre Technology**, this directive mandates the submission of a **Module IV Technical Report** — a structured, documented evaluation of a real-world or simulated tyre-testing/performance-assessment activity — to the course faculty for assessment, grading, and archival.

### 1.1 Conceptual Context within Module IV

Module IV of PBPOT504 (Tyre Technology) under the KTU 2024 NEP-aligned syllabus primarily covers the **performance evaluation, testing methodologies, and quality assessment of pneumatic tyres**. The submission directive therefore logically refers to a **technical report** arising from:

- Tyre performance testing (rolling resistance, wet/dry grip, braking, noise, etc.)
- Tyre durability and endurance evaluation
- Tyre inspection and defect analysis
- Comparative study of tyre specifications
- Field/simulated test observations

> [!NOTE]
> **For First-Time Learners:** Think of this directive the same way a mechanical engineering student in a *Machine Shop* course submits a *Workshop Report* documenting a turning or milling experiment. Here, in Tyre Technology, you submit a **Tyre Performance / Testing Report** documenting experiments or evaluations performed on tyres as per Module IV learning outcomes.

### 1.2 Purpose of the Report Submission

The report submission serves **four** primary academic purposes within the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework:

1. **Evidence of Learning (CO Attainment):** Each report maps to a specific **Course Outcome (CO)** — typically **CO4** of Module IV (e.g., "Evaluate tyre performance using standard testing protocols").
2. **Skill Demonstration:** Demonstrates the student's ability to *record, analyze, and interpret* technical data.
3. **Communication Competency:** Assesses the student's written technical communication — a KTU graduate attribute.
4. **Continuous Assessment Weightage:** Contributes to the **CIE component** of the final grade (typically 50 marks internal out of 150 total, with assignment/report components carrying defined weightage).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Assessment Reminder:** Per KTU 2024 regulations, every report submitted must be:
> - **Handwritten or printed** (as instructed by faculty),
> - **Signed by the student**,
> - **Submitted on or before the deadline** announced by the course faculty,
> - **Acknowledged via faculty signature** or official receipt mechanism.

### 1.3 Standard Report Categories in Tyre Technology (Module IV)

| Report Type | Typical Content | Weightage Indicator |
|---|---|---|
| **Tyre Performance Test Report** | Rolling resistance, braking distance, wet traction | High |
| **Tyre Defect / Inspection Report** | Visual inspection, non-destructive testing observations | Medium |
| **Comparative Specification Report** | Comparison of 2–3 tyre brands/models | Medium |
| **Field Study Report** | Industry visit / OEM data interpretation | Medium |
| **Mini-Project Report** | Small-scale experimental study | High |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework: Why Reports Are Mandatory in Tyre Technology

Tyre Technology is fundamentally an **applied engineering discipline**. Unlike pure-theory subjects, its mastery depends on the student's ability to:

- **Observe** real tyre behaviour under controlled conditions,
- **Measure** performance parameters using industry-standard instruments,
- **Analyze** raw data into meaningful engineering conclusions,
- **Document** the entire process in a structured, reproducible format.

The "submit the report to the faculty" directive is the **terminal node** of this pedagogical cycle. Without submission, the learning loop remains incomplete.

### 2.2 The Pedagogical Logic (Step-by-Step)

1. **Step 1 – Instruction:** Faculty defines the Module IV testing/evaluation activity.
2. **Step 2 – Execution:** Student performs the test, collects data, captures observations.
3. **Step 3 – Documentation:** Student writes the report in the prescribed format.
4. **Step 4 – Submission:** Report is physically/digitally handed over to faculty.
5. **Step 5 – Assessment:** Faculty evaluates using a rubric (clarity, accuracy, analysis, presentation).
6. **Step 6 – Feedback:** Faculty returns the report with marks and feedback, completing the CO attainment loop.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Module IV Test Report Metrics

While the directive itself is administrative, the **report content** typically includes calculations using the following standard Tyre Technology formulas. These are the most frequently evaluated components in the viva/practical examination:

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Convention:** $F$ = Force, $r$ = tyre rolling radius, $v$ = vehicle velocity, $\rho$ = air density, $C_d$ = drag coefficient, $A_f$ = frontal area, $M$ = vehicle mass, $g$ = gravitational acceleration ($\mathbf{9.81 \ m/s^2}$).

| S.No. | Parameter | Formula | Units | Real-World Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Rolling Resistance Force** | $F_{rr} = C_{rr} \cdot M \cdot g$ | Newtons (N) | Fuel economy estimation |
| 2 | **Rolling Resistance Power** | $P_{rr} = F_{rr} \cdot v$ | Watts (W) | Engine load calculation |
| 3 | **Aerodynamic Drag Force** | $F_d = \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot \rho \cdot v^2 \cdot C_d \cdot A_f$ | Newtons (N) | High-speed performance |
| 4 | **Braking Distance** | $d_b = \dfrac{v^2}{2 \cdot \mu \cdot g}$ | Metres (m) | Tyre-road friction ($\mu$) |
| 5 | **Tyre Load Index Conversion** | $LI \rightarrow LC$ (via ISO table) | kg | Load-carrying capacity |
| 6 | **Tread Wear Rate** | $W_r = \dfrac{TWI_0 - TWI_t}{d}$ | mm/1000 km | Tyre life prediction |
| 7 | **Slip Ratio** | $s = \dfrac{v - \omega r}{v} \times 100\%$ | % | Traction/braking efficiency |
| 8 | **Contact Patch Pressure** | $P_{cp} = \dfrac{F_z}{A_{cp}}$ | kPa | Load distribution analysis |

### 2.4 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Automotive OEMs** (e.g., MRF, Apollo, CEAT) require such structured reports during **product qualification**.
- **Testing agencies** (ARAI, iCAT) generate them for **homologation**.
- **R&D departments** use them to validate **CAE simulation** outputs.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** The "submit the report" directive is the **gateway** to securing CIE marks. Students often lose marks not because the report content is poor, but because of **improper submission procedure** (wrong format, missed deadline, missing signature, missing plagiarism declaration).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module Note:** The directive *"Students should submit the report to the faculty"* is a **procedural/administrative instruction** and does not, by itself, contain derivable equations. However, the **report it refers to** contains technical calculations drawn from Module IV. Below is the exhaustive derivation of the **two most frequently assessed** formulas encountered in such reports, since these represent the analytical core of a Tyre Technology submission.

### 3.1 Derivation: Braking Distance as a Function of Tyre Friction

> **Problem Setup:** A passenger car (mass $M = 1500 \ kg$) equipped with the test tyres is travelling at $v = 90 \ km/h$ on a dry asphalt surface. The tyre-road friction coefficient is $\mu = 0.75$. Compute the theoretical braking distance and the energy dissipated by the tyres.

#### Step 1 — Velocity Conversion (SI Standardization)

KTU examiner evaluation requires the velocity to be in SI units (m/s) before any calculation. The conversion is:

$$v_{SI} = v_{km/h} \times \frac{1000}{3600} = v_{km/h} \times \frac{5}{18}$$

$$v_{SI} = 90 \times \frac{5}{18} = 25 \ m/s$$

#### Step 2 — Application of Work–Energy Theorem

The kinetic energy of the vehicle must be fully dissipated by the friction work done by the tyres on the road. Setting the two equal:

$$\frac{1}{2} \cdot M \cdot v^2 = F_{friction} \cdot d_b$$

The total friction force available from all four tyres is:

$$F_{friction} = \mu \cdot M \cdot g$$

#### Step 3 — Substituting and Solving for $d_b$

Substituting the friction expression into the energy balance:

$$\frac{1}{2} \cdot M \cdot v^2 = \mu \cdot M \cdot g \cdot d_b$$

The vehicle mass $M$ appears on **both** sides and therefore cancels out — a key insight that demonstrates braking distance is **mass-independent** under constant friction:

$$d_b = \frac{v^2}{2 \cdot \mu \cdot g}$$

#### Step 4 — Numerical Evaluation

Inserting the known values:

$$d_b = \frac{(25)^2}{2 \times 0.75 \times 9.81}$$

$$d_b = \frac{625}{14.715}$$

$$d_b = 42.47 \ m$$

#### Step 5 — Energy Dissipated by the Tyres

$$E_{dissipated} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot M \cdot v^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 1500 \times (25)^2$$

$$E_{dissipated} = 750 \times 625 = 468{,}750 \ J = 468.75 \ kJ$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Insight:** The KTU board examiner expects students to explicitly state that $M$ cancels in Step 3 and to comment on the engineering implication — namely that **braking distance is independent of vehicle mass** under idealized friction conditions.

### 3.2 Derivation: Rolling Resistance Power Loss

> **Problem Setup:** The same vehicle ($M = 1500 \ kg$) travels at $v = 90 \ km/h = 25 \ m/s$ with a coefficient of rolling resistance $C_{rr} = 0.012$ (typical for a radial passenger car tyre on asphalt). Compute the rolling resistance force and the power loss.

#### Step 1 — Rolling Resistance Force

$$F_{rr} = C_{rr} \cdot M \cdot g$$

$$F_{rr} = 0.012 \times 1500 \times 9.81$$

$$F_{rr} = 176.58 \ N$$

#### Step 2 — Rolling Resistance Power

$$P_{rr} = F_{rr} \cdot v$$

$$P_{rr} = 176.58 \times 25$$

$$P_{rr} = 4414.5 \ W \approx 4.41 \ kW$$

#### Step 3 — Fuel Consumption Equivalence

Using the approximate conversion factor of $1 \ kWh \approx 0.084 \ kg$ of petrol (lower heating value basis):

$$\dot{m}_{fuel,extra} = \frac{4.41 \times 0.084}{v_{km/h} / 3600 \times 1000 \ km} \approx 0.37 \ kg \ /\ 100 \ km$$

> [!TIP]
> **Report-Writing Note:** Whenever such derivations appear in a Module IV report, the student should always include the **assumption block** (e.g., level road, steady-state, no wind, no wheel slip) at the top of the calculation section. KTU examiners allocate up to 1 mark specifically for **stated assumptions**.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Report Quality Rubric (KTU-Aligned)

The faculty, when evaluating the submitted report, typically uses the following assessment matrix. Understanding this helps the student **target maximum marks**:

| Evaluation Criterion | Weightage | KTU Examiner Expectation |
|---|---|---|
| **Title Page & Index** | 5% | Course code, title, name, roll no, date, faculty name |
| **Aim & Objectives** | 10% | Clear, measurable, aligned to Module IV COs |
| **Theory / Background** | 15% | Brief, relevant, properly cited |
| **Apparatus / Methodology** | 10% | Standard test names (e.g., ISO 28580, ECE R30) |
| **Data & Observations** | 20% | Tabulated, units included, raw + processed |
| **Calculations & Graphs** | 20% | Stepwise, with formulae, with sample calculations |
| **Results & Discussion** | 10% | Comparison with standards, sources of error |
| **Conclusion & References** | 5% | Concise conclusion, proper bibliography |
| **Presentation / Viva** | 5% | Neatness, grammar, oral defense readiness |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Flow: The Report Submission Lifecycle

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the complete end-to-end flow of how a Module IV report is generated, submitted, evaluated, and returned within the KTU 2024 CIE framework.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Faculty Issues Module IV Assignment] --> B[Student Performs Tyre Test Activity]
    B --> C[Data Collection and Observation Recording]
    C --> D[Student Drafts Report in Prescribed Format]
    D --> E{Quality Check by Student}
    E -->|Needs Revision| D
    E -->|Ready for Submission| F[Student Signs the Report Cover Page]
    F --> G[Physical or LMS-Based Submission to Faculty]
    G --> H[Faculty Receives and Acknowledges Receipt]
    H --> I[Faculty Evaluates Using Rubric]
    I --> J[Marks Entered in KTU University CIE Register]
    J --> K[Report Returned with Feedback]
    K --> L[Student Reviews Feedback for CO Attainment]
    L --> M[Module IV CO Closure and Progression to Module V]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Standard Report Structure (Module IV Tyre Technology)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Cover Page] --> R2[Certificate / Declaration Page]
    R2 --> R3[Table of Contents]
    R3 --> R4[Objective of the Study]
    R4 --> R5[Theoretical Background]
    R5 --> R6[Apparatus and Test Standards]
    R6 --> R7[Procedure / Methodology]
    R7 --> R8[Data and Observations Table]
    R8 --> R9[Sample Calculations]
    R9 --> R10[Graphs and Plots]
    R10 --> R11[Results and Discussion]
    R11 --> R12[Sources of Error and Precautions]
    R12 --> R13[Conclusion]
    R13 --> R14[References and Bibliography]
    R14 --> R15[Appendices Raw Data Sheets]
```

### 4.3 Decision Diagram: Submission Mode Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1{Faculty Instruction on Submission Mode} -->|Hard Copy| S2[Print Report on A4 Sheets]
    S1 -->|Soft Copy| S3[Convert to PDF Format]
    S1 -->|LMS Upload| S4[Upload to KTU LMS Portal]
    S2 --> S5[Bind or Staple Properly]
    S3 --> S5
    S4 --> S6[Await Acknowledgment from Faculty]
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7[Submission Complete]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** All three diagrams above are rendered as **block-level functional architecture flows** rather than physical drawings, in accordance with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 safeguard for complex physical schematics. They depict the *process* and *structure* of the report submission system rather than any physical tyre component.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 1, 3 Marks]**
> **Q1.** What is the significance of submitting a technical report at the end of Module IV activities in the Tyre Technology course?
>
> **Model Answer:**
> The submission of a technical report at the conclusion of Module IV activities is significant for three principal reasons within the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework:
> 1. **Course Outcome (CO) Attainment Evidence:** It provides documented proof that the student has achieved the cognitive and psychomotor learning outcomes defined for Module IV — typically CO4, which focuses on the evaluation of tyre performance using standard testing protocols.
> 2. **Continuous Internal Evaluation (CIE) Component:** The report constitutes a graded component of the CIE, contributing defined marks toward the internal assessment total of 50 marks.
> 3. **Development of Graduate Attributes:** It nurtures the professional competencies of technical documentation, data interpretation, and structured communication — all of which are essential KTU graduate attributes required for engineering practice.

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question 2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q2.** List any **six** essential components that a Module IV Tyre Technology report must contain to be considered complete as per KTU evaluation standards.
>
> **Model Answer:** The six essential components are:
> 1. **Title page** with course code, student details, and faculty name.
> 2. **Aim and objectives** aligned to the Module IV learning outcomes.
> 3. **Theoretical background** including relevant test standards (e.g., ISO 28580, ECE R30).
> 4. **Apparatus and methodology** section describing the test setup.
> 5. **Tabulated data and observations** with proper SI units.
> 6. **Conclusion** summarizing the key findings and a properly formatted **References** section.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern, 14 Marks]**
>
> **Question A (Choice 1):**
> **(a)** Describe the complete structure and submission procedure of a Module IV technical report in Tyre Technology. In your description, clearly state the importance of each section in the context of KTU 2024 Continuous Internal Evaluation (CIE) norms. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A passenger car of mass $M = 1200 \ kg$ is fitted with four identical radial tyres. The vehicle is tested on a dry asphalt surface at $v = 72 \ km/h$ and brought to rest using maximum braking. The tyre-road friction coefficient is $\mu = 0.70$ and the coefficient of rolling resistance is $C_{rr} = 0.010$. Calculate: **(i)** the theoretical braking distance, **(ii)** the rolling resistance power loss, and **(iii)** the total energy dissipated by the tyres during braking. **(7 Marks)**

### 5.3 Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — Report Structure and Submission Procedure (7 Marks)

A Module IV technical report in Tyre Technology must follow a standardized structure to ensure uniform evaluation by the faculty. The structure and its purpose are:

| Section | Purpose | CIE Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Title Page** | Identifies the report uniquely with course code, title, student name, roll number, faculty name, and date. | Establishes academic authenticity. |
| **Aim & Objectives** | Clearly states the testing/evaluation goal aligned to Module IV COs. | Provides the *why* of the work. |
| **Theory** | Concise background citing relevant standards (ISO 28580 for rolling resistance, ECE R30 for braking, etc.). | Demonstrates conceptual understanding. |
| **Apparatus & Procedure** | Lists instruments, software, and step-by-step methodology. | Demonstrates reproducibility. |
| **Observations & Data** | Tabulated raw data with SI units. | Forms the empirical foundation. |
| **Calculations** | Stepwise derivation using standard formulas. | Tests applied knowledge. |
| **Results & Discussion** | Interpretation, comparison with standards, error analysis. | Tests analytical thinking. |
| **Conclusion & References** | Summary of findings and bibliographic citations. | Demonstrates academic integrity. |

> The **submission procedure** involves: (i) verification of all sections, (ii) signature on the declaration page, (iii) physical binding/stapling or LMS upload as instructed, and (iv) timely submission to the faculty for entry into the CIE register. **[Award: 7 marks for full coverage.]**

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**

$$M = 1200 \ kg, \quad v = 72 \ km/h, \quad \mu = 0.70, \quad C_{rr} = 0.010, \quad g = 9.81 \ m/s^2$$

**Step 1 — Velocity Conversion to SI Units:**

$$v = 72 \times \frac{5}{18} = 20 \ m/s$$

**Step 2 — (i) Braking Distance Calculation:**

$$d_b = \frac{v^2}{2 \cdot \mu \cdot g}$$

$$d_b = \frac{(20)^2}{2 \times 0.70 \times 9.81}$$

$$d_b = \frac{400}{13.734}$$

$$d_b = 29.12 \ m$$

**Step 3 — (ii) Rolling Resistance Power Loss:**

$$F_{rr} = C_{rr} \cdot M \cdot g = 0.010 \times 1200 \times 9.81 = 117.72 \ N$$

$$P_{rr} = F_{rr} \cdot v = 117.72 \times 20 = 2354.4 \ W \approx 2.35 \ kW$$

**Step 4 — (iii) Total Energy Dissipated During Braking:**

$$E_{brake} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot M \cdot v^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 1200 \times (20)^2 = 600 \times 400 = 240{,}000 \ J = 240 \ kJ$$

**Final Tabulated Answer:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Braking Distance $d_b$ | $\mathbf{29.12 \ m}$ |
| Rolling Resistance Power $P_{rr}$ | $\mathbf{2.35 \ kW}$ |
| Energy Dissipated $E_{brake}$ | $\mathbf{240 \ kJ}$ |

**[Award: 7 marks for stepwise correct evaluation with units and assumptions.]**

---

> **Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative):**
> **(a)** Explain, with a suitable block diagram, the complete lifecycle of a Module IV report — from assignment issue to CO attainment closure. Describe the role of the faculty and the student at each stage. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** A truck tyre is tested on a chassis dynamometer. The rolling resistance force measured is $F_{rr} = 380 \ N$ at a constant speed of $v = 80 \ km/h$. The vertical load on the tyre is $F_z = 4000 \ N$. Calculate: **(i)** the effective coefficient of rolling resistance, **(ii)** the rolling resistance power loss in kW, and **(iii)** the equivalent fuel energy consumption per 100 km, assuming a thermal efficiency of $\eta_{th} = 30\%$. **(7 Marks)**

### 5.4 Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — Report Lifecycle Explanation (7 Marks)

The lifecycle consists of **seven sequential stages**, each with defined roles for the faculty and the student:

1. **Assignment Issue (Faculty Role):** Faculty defines the Module IV test/evaluation activity and the report submission deadline.
2. **Execution (Student Role):** The student performs the test, observes, and records data systematically.
3. **Drafting (Student Role):** The student writes the report in the prescribed KTU format, including aim, theory, data, calculations, and conclusion.
4. **Self-Review (Student Role):** The student verifies calculations, units, references, and presentation quality.
5. **Submission (Student Role):** The student signs, binds, and submits the report to the faculty on or before the deadline.
6. **Evaluation (Faculty Role):** The faculty evaluates using the rubric, enters marks in the CIE register, and provides written feedback.
7. **CO Closure (Joint Role):** The marks and feedback collectively serve as evidence of CO4 attainment, closing the Module IV learning loop and enabling progression to Module V.

> A suitable **flowchart** for this lifecycle has been provided in **Section 4.1** of these notes. **[Award: 7 marks.]**

#### Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks)

**Given Data:**

$$F_{rr} = 380 \ N, \quad v = 80 \ km/h, \quad F_z = 4000 \ N, \quad \eta_{th} = 0.30$$

**Step 1 — Velocity Conversion:**

$$v = 80 \times \frac{5}{18} = 22.22 \ m/s$$

**Step 2 — (i) Effective Coefficient of Rolling Resistance:**

The effective coefficient is defined as the ratio of rolling resistance force to vertical load:

$$C_{rr,eff} = \frac{F_{rr}}{F_z} = \frac{380}{4000} = 0.095$$

**Step 3 — (ii) Rolling Resistance Power Loss:**

$$P_{rr} = F_{rr} \cdot v = 380 \times 22.22 = 8443.6 \ W \approx 8.44 \ kW$$

**Step 4 — (iii) Equivalent Fuel Energy per 100 km:**

The mechanical energy consumed per 100 km is:

$$E_{mech,100} = P_{rr} \times t = P_{rr} \times \frac{100 \times 1000}{v_{km/h} \times 1000 / 3600}$$

$$E_{mech,100} = 8.44 \times \frac{100}{80} \times 3600 \times \frac{1}{3600} = 8.44 \times \frac{100}{80} = 10.55 \ kWh$$

Accounting for thermal efficiency:

$$E_{fuel,100} = \frac{E_{mech,100}}{\eta_{th}} = \frac{10.55}{0.30} = 35.17 \ kWh$$

Converting to litres of diesel (density $\rho_d \approx 0.85 \ kg/L$, LCV $\approx 43.1 \ MJ/kg$):

$$V_{fuel} = \frac{35.17 \times 3.6 \ MJ}{43.1 \ MJ/kg \times 0.85 \ kg/L} = \frac{126.6}{36.6} \approx 3.46 \ L / 100 \ km$$

**Final Tabulated Answer:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Effective $C_{rr}$ | $\mathbf{0.095}$ |
| Power Loss $P_{rr}$ | $\mathbf{8.44 \ kW}$ |
| Fuel Consumption Equivalent | $\mathbf{3.46 \ L / 100 \ km}$ |

**[Award: 7 marks for full stepwise solution with stated assumptions.]**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency (–1 to –2 marks):** Forgetting to convert $km/h$ to $m/s$ is the single most frequent error. KTU examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 2. **Missing Assumptions (–1 mark):** Always state assumptions (level road, steady-state, no wind, no wheel slip) before calculations.
> 3. **Improper Report Submission (Up to 100% loss of CIE marks):** Submitting without signature, missing declaration page, or missing the deadline can result in *zero* marks for the report component.
> 4. **Lack of Standard Citation (–1 mark):** Always cite the relevant test standard (ISO 28580, ECE R30, SAE J1269) when describing tyre tests.
> 5. **Viva Inability (–2 to –3 marks):** During the viva, inability to explain one's own calculations or justify a conclusion is treated as a serious deficiency.
